100 PM | 5–25 Developers | | **Large** | 100 KLOC – 1 MLOC | 100 – 1000 PM | 25–100 Developers | | **Very Large** | > 1 MLOC | > 1000 PM | 100+ Developers | > [!NOTE] > The **Boehm / COCOMO II** thresholds typically used in KTU references place the boundary at **10 KLOC** (small) and **100 KLOC** (medium). ### Dimension 3 — By Criticality (Consequence of Failure) Criticality dictates testing rigor, documentation, and process maturity. | Criticality Level | Consequence of Failure | Standards Applicable | |---|---|---| | **Safety-Critical** | Loss of life | DO-178C (Avionics), IEC 62304 (Medical), ISO 26262 (Automotive) | | **Mission-Critical** | Mission failure, severe financial loss | NASA NPR 7150.2, MIL-STD-882 | | **Business-Critical** | Major business disruption | PCI-DSS, SOX, GDPR | | **Business-Supportive** | Operational inconvenience | Internal SLAs | | **Non-Critical / Convenience** | Minor inconvenience | None | ### Dimension 4 — By Development Approach (Lifecycle Category) This is the *managerial* dimension — what lifecycle model fits the category. | Project Category | Best-Fit Lifecycle | Reason | |---|---|---| | Well-defined, stable requirements | **Waterfall** | Sequential, predictable | | Evolving, unclear requirements | **Agile / Scrum** | Iterative, adaptive | | High-risk, complex systems | **Spiral** | Risk-driven prototyping | | Safety-critical, verifiable | **V-Model** | Test parallelism to development | | Mixed (some stable, some evolving) | **Hybrid** | Tailored blend | ### Dimension 5 — By Complexity Computed from architectural and algorithmic attributes: $$ C_{\text{project}} = f(\text{Modules},\ \text{Interfaces},\ \text{Algorithmic Difficulty},\ \text{Data Volume}) $$ | Complexity Class | Indicators | |---|---| | **Simple** | < 10 modules, linear algorithms, single user | | **Moderate** | 10–50 modules, conditional logic, multi-user | | **Complex** | 50–200 modules, distributed, real-time constraints | | **Very Complex** | > 200 modules, concurrent, distributed, safety-critical | ### Dimension 6 — By Risk Profile (Probability × Impact) $$ R = P \times I \quad \text{where} \quad P, I \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} $$ Therefore: $$ R \in \{1, 2, 3, \dots, 25\} $$ | Risk Score | Category | Action | |---|---|---| | 1 – 5 | **Low Risk** | Standard monitoring | | 6 – 12 | **Moderate Risk** | Weekly reviews, mitigation plan | | 13 – 19 | **High Risk** | Daily stand-ups, escalation matrix | | 20 – 25 | **Extreme Risk** | Executive sponsorship, possible cancellation | ## 2.2 The CMM-Based Process Categorization (People-CMM / CMMI) The Software Engineering Institute (SEI) categorizes an *organization's process maturity* into **5 levels**, which directly impacts how any project is managed: | CMMI Level | Name | Process Categorization | |---|---|---| | **Level 1** | Initial | Chaotic, reactive, hero-based | | **Level 2** | Managed | Basic project tracking, repeatable successes | | **Level 3** | Defined | Organization-wide standards, proactive | | **Level 4** | Quantitatively Managed | Measured and controlled using metrics | | **Level 5** | Optimizing | Continuous improvement via feedback loops | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Categorize the following project"*, always cite **three dimensions minimum** — domain, size, and criticality — for full marks. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Avionics:** Categorized as *safety-critical + real-time + very complex* → requires DO-178C Level A and V-Model. - **Mobile Banking App:** *Business-critical + application + small/medium* → Agile + PCI-DSS compliance. - **AI Diagnostic Tool:** *Safety-critical + AI/ML + complex* → Hybrid (Agile + V-Model verification tail). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Effort-Based Project Category In COCOMO II (the model most referenced in KTU syllabus), the **basic effort estimate** is given by: $$ E = a \times \left( \text{KLOC} \right)^{b} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EM}_{i} $$ Where: - $E$ = Effort in **Person-Months (PM)** - $a$ = Base productivity constant (1.4 for organic, 3.4 for semi-detached, 3.6 for embedded) - $b$ = Scale exponent (1.05 for organic, 1.12 for semi-detached, 1.20 for embedded) - $\text{KLOC}$ = Thousand Lines of Code - $\text{EM}_{i}$ = Effort Multipliers for cost drivers ### Step-by-Step Categorization Derivation **Step 1 — Measure Size** Let the measured source code be $S$ lines. $$ \text{KLOC} = \frac{S}{1000} $$ **Step 2 — Determine Project Category from Size** $$ \text{Category} = \begin{cases} \text{Trivial} & \text{if } \text{KLOC} < 1 \\ \text{Small} & \text{if } 1 \leq \text{KLOC} < 10 \\ \text{Medium} & \text{if } 10 \leq \text{KLOC} < 100 \\ \text{Large} & \text{if } 100 \leq \text{KLOC} < 1000 \\ \text{Very Large} & \text{if } \text{KLOC} \geq 1000 \end{cases} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Effort Using COCOMO** For an **organic** project (small, in-house, familiar), with $a = 1.4$, $b = 1.05$, and $\prod \text{EM}_{i} = 1$: $$ E = 1.4 \times (\text{KLOC})^{1.05} $$ **Step 4 — Categorize Effort Magnitude** $$ \text{Effort Category} = \begin{cases} \text{Minor} & \text{if } E < 6 \ \text{PM} \\ \text{Moderate} & \text{if } 6 \leq E < 24 \ \text{PM} \\ \text{Major} & \text{if } 24 \leq E < 100 \ \text{PM} \\ \text{Mega} & \text{if } E \geq 100 \ \text{PM} \end{cases} $$ **Step 5 — Derive Team Size via Productivity** Let productivity $P$ be measured in **KLOC per Person-Month**: $$ P = \frac{\text{KLOC}}{E} $$ Then the required team size is: $$ N = \frac{E}{D \times \text{Availability}} $$ Where: - $D$ = Project duration in months - $\text{Availability} = 0.6$ to $0.8$ (accounting for holidays, meetings, overhead) ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A project is estimated at **25 KLOC**. Classify it along the size and effort dimensions using COCOMO organic parameters. ### Solution — Step 1: Size Category Given $\text{KLOC} = 25$. Since $10 \leq 25 < 100$: $$ \boxed{\text{Size Category} = \text{Medium}} $$ ### Solution — Step 2: Effort Calculation Using organic mode ($a = 1.4$, $b = 1.05$), assuming $\prod \text{EM}_{i} = 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} E &= 1.4 \times (25)^{1.05} \\ &= 1.4 \times \exp(1.05 \times \ln(25)) \\ &= 1.4 \times \exp(1.05 \times 3.2189) \\ &= 1.4 \times \exp(3.3798) \\ &= 1.4 \times 29.36 \\ &= 41.10 \ \text{PM} \end{aligned} $$ ### Solution — Step 3: Effort Category Since $24 \leq 41.10 < 100$: $$ \boxed{\text{Effort Category} = \text{Major}} $$ ### Solution — Step 4: Productivity $$ \begin{aligned} P &= \frac{25 \ \text{KLOC}}{41.10 \ \text{PM}} \\ &= 0.608 \ \text{KLOC/PM} \end{aligned} $$ ### Solution — Step 5: Team Size for 12-Month Schedule Assuming availability factor $= 0.75$: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \frac{41.10}{12 \times 0.75} \\ &= \frac{41.10}{9} \\ &= 4.57 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{N \approx 5 \ \text{Developers}} $$ ## 3.3 The Master Categorization Decision Matrix For any incoming project, the SPM lead populates this matrix to *formally* categorize it: | # | Question Answered | Input Metric | Output Category | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | What does it do? | Application Domain | System / App / Real-Time / etc. | | 2 | How big is it? | KLOC or FP | Trivial → Very Large | | 3 | What happens if it fails? | Consequence Analysis | Safety → Non-Critical | | 4 | How complex is the architecture? | Modules, Interfaces | Simple → Very Complex | | 5 | How risky is it? | P × I Score | Low → Extreme | | 6 | What is the process maturity of the org? | CMMI Assessment | Level 1 → 5 | | 7 | What lifecycle fits? | Requirements Stability | Waterfall / Agile / Spiral / V | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Composite Category String:** > A complete categorization is expressed as a *tuple* — for example: > `"Real-Time + Large + Safety-Critical + Complex + High-Risk + CMMI L3 → Spiral + V-Model hybrid"` # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Software Categorization ```mermaid graph TD A["Software Project"] --> B["By Domain"] A --> C["By Size"] A --> D["By Criticality"] A --> E["By Complexity"] A --> F["By Risk"] A --> G["By Process Maturity"] B --> B1["System Software"] B --> B2["Application Software"] B --> B3["Embedded Software"] B --> B4["Real-Time Software"] B --> B5["Web and Cloud Software"] B --> B6["AI and ML Software"] B --> B7["Scientific Software"] C --> C1["Trivial Less Than 1 KLOC"] C --> C2["Small 1 to 10 KLOC"] C --> C3["Medium 10 to 100 KLOC"] C --> C4["Large 100 to 1000 KLOC"] C --> C5["Very Large Greater Than 1 MLOC"] D --> D1["Safety-Critical"] D --> D2["Mission-Critical"] D --> D3["Business-Critical"] D --> D4["Business-Supportive"] D --> D5["Non-Critical"] E --> E1["Simple"] E --> E2["Moderate"] E --> E3["Complex"] E --> E4["Very Complex"] F --> F1["Low 1 to 5"] F --> F2["Moderate 6 to 12"] F --> F3["High 13 to 19"] F --> F4["Extreme 20 to 25"] G --> G1["CMMI Level 1 Initial"] G --> G2["CMMI Level 2 Managed"] G --> G3["CMMI Level 3 Defined"] G --> G4["CMMI Level 4 Quantitative"] G --> G5["CMMI Level 5 Optimizing"] ``` ## 4.2 Categorization-Driven Lifecycle Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start["New Project Intake"] --> q1{"Are requirements
fully defined?"} q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Is it safety or
mission critical?"} q1 -- No --> q3{"Is risk the
dominant driver?"} q2 -- Yes --> vm["Select V-Model
with rigorous verification"] q2 -- No --> wf["Select Waterfall
predictive sequential"] q3 -- Yes --> sp["Select Spiral
risk-driven prototyping"] q3 -- No --> ag["Select Agile or Scrum
iterative adaptive"] vm --> cls["Classify Size, Criticality,
and Complexity metrics"] wf --> cls sp --> cls ag --> cls cls --> plan["Generate Estimation,
Schedule, and Risk Plan"] ``` ## 4.3 CMMI Maturity Levels — Pyramid View ```mermaid graph TB L5["Level 5 Optimizing
Continuous Improvement"] L4["Level 4 Quantitatively Managed
Metric-Driven Control"] L3["Level 3 Defined
Organization Standards"] L2["Level 2 Managed
Repeatable Project Tracking"] L1["Level 1 Initial
Chaotic and Heroic"] L5 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 ``` ## 4.4 Risk Categorization Heat Map ```mermaid graph LR P1["Impact 5 Extreme"] --- P2["Impact 4 Major"] --- P3["Impact 3 Moderate"] --- P4["Impact 2 Minor"] --- P5["Impact 1 Negligible"] Q1["Prob 1 Rare"] --- Q2["Prob 2 Unlikely"] --- Q3["Prob 3 Possible"] --- Q4["Prob 4 Likely"] --- Q5["Prob 5 Almost Certain"] zone1["Extreme Zone
R = 20 to 25"] -.-> P1 zone2["High Zone
R = 13 to 19"] -.-> P2 zone3["Moderate Zone
R = 6 to 12"] -.-> P3 zone4["Low Zone
R = 1 to 5"] -.-> P4 ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Categorization Engine ```mermaid graph LR A["Project Intake Module
Captures Requirements and Domain"] --> B["Size Analyzer
KLOC and FP Calculator"] A --> C["Criticality Classifier
Consequence Analysis Engine"] A --> D["Complexity Evaluator
Module and Interface Counter"] A --> E["Risk Profiler
Probability Impact Matrix"] B --> F["Category Aggregator
Combines All Dimensions"] C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G["Lifecycle Selector
Waterfall or Agile or Spiral or V"] F --> H["Estimation Engine
COCOMO II Formulas"] F --> I["Compliance Mapper
DO-178C, IEC 62304, ISO 26262"] G --> J["Project Charter Output"] H --> J I --> J ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **Define software project categorization. List any four dimensions used to categorize a software project.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1 Mark):** Software project categorization is the systematic classification of software products and their development projects into defined groups based on attributes such as domain, size, criticality, complexity, and risk, in order to select an appropriate management strategy. **Four Dimensions (2 Marks — ½ mark each):** 1. **By Application Domain** — System, Application, Embedded, Real-Time, Web, AI/ML. 2. **By Size** — Trivial, Small, Medium, Large, Very Large (measured in KLOC). 3. **By Criticality** — Safety, Mission, Business, Business-Supportive, Non-Critical. 4. **By Risk Profile** — Low, Moderate, High, Extreme (computed as Probability × Impact). --- ### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Differentiate between safety-critical and mission-critical software with one example each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Aspect | Safety-Critical | Mission-Critical | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Software whose failure causes loss of human life or severe injury | Software whose failure causes mission failure or severe financial/organizational loss | | **Consequence** | Catastrophic — human fatality | Major — financial or operational disaster, but no direct loss of life | | **Examples** | Pacemaker firmware, Aircraft flight control (DO-178C Level A) | NASA's ground control, Banking transaction engine | | **Standards** | IEC 62304, DO-178C, ISO 26262 | NASA NPR 7150.2, MIL-STD-882 | **Examples (1 Mark):** Safety — *Aircraft autopilot*; Mission — *Stock exchange order-matching engine*. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module 1 Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`** #### (a) Explain in detail the categorization of software based on application domain. Highlight the unique management challenges for each category. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Answer (a): Software can be categorized into the following application domains, each presenting distinct managerial challenges: **1. System Software (1 Mark)** Manages hardware resources. Examples: Operating Systems, Compilers, Device Drivers. *Management Challenge:* Tight coupling with hardware architecture demands deep hardware-software co-design expertise. Testing requires hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) setups. Estimation is hard because bugs often surface only on rare hardware configurations. **2. Application Software (1 Mark)** Solves user or business problems. Examples: ERP, CRM, MS Word. *Management Challenge:* Requirements volatility is the chief challenge — users often do not know what they want. Agile methodologies are preferred. Stakeholder management dominates the project manager's time. **3. Embedded Software (1 Mark)** Resides inside non-computing devices. Examples: Washing machine firmware, IoT sensors. *Management Challenge:* Severe resource constraints (memory, CPU, power). Real-time operating systems (RTOS) are mandatory. Cross-functional teams (mechanical + electrical + software) are needed. **4. Real-Time Software (1 Mark)** Must respond within deterministic time deadlines. Examples: Air traffic control, Pacemakers. *Management Challenge:* Hard deadlines mean that no "late delivery" is acceptable. Formal verification methods (model checking, theorem proving) are often mandatory alongside traditional testing. **5. Web / Cloud Software (1 Mark)** Runs on remote servers, accessed via browsers or APIs. Examples: Gmail, AWS services. *Management Challenge:* Scalability under unpredictable load is the primary challenge. DevOps, containerization (Docker/Kubernetes), and continuous deployment pipelines are essential. **6. AI / ML Software (1 Mark)** Learns from data and makes predictions. Examples: ChatGPT, fraud detection engines. *Management Challenge:* Non-deterministic outputs make traditional testing inadequate. Data quality, model drift, and ethical/regulatory concerns (GDPR, AI Act) dominate the risk profile. **7. Scientific & Engineering Software (1 Mark)** Performs heavy numerical computation. Examples: MATLAB simulations, CFD solvers. *Management Challenge:* Algorithm complexity and floating-point accuracy are paramount. Validation is done against known analytical benchmarks, not user acceptance tests. --- #### (b) A software project is estimated at **45 KLOC**. Using **COCOMO basic (organic mode)** with cost drivers product = 1.15, personnel = 0.88, platform = 1.0, classify the project by size and effort, and compute the recommended team size for a **15-month** schedule. Assume availability factor = 0.7. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Answer (b): **Step 1 — Size Classification (1 Mark):** $$ \text{KLOC} = 45 $$ Since $10 \leq 45 < 100$: $$ \boxed{\text{Size Category} = \text{Medium}} $$ **Step 2 — Effort Multiplier Product (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \prod \text{EM}_{i} &= 1.15 \times 0.88 \times 1.0 \\ &= 1.012 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Effort Calculation (2 Marks):** Using organic parameters $a = 1.4$, $b = 1.05$: $$ \begin{aligned} E &= 1.4 \times (45)^{1.05} \times 1.012 \\ &= 1.4 \times 52.49 \times 1.012 \\ &= 74.37 \ \text{PM} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Effort Category (1 Mark):** Since $24 \leq 74.37 < 100$: $$ \boxed{\text{Effort Category} = \text{Major}} $$ **Step 5 — Team Size (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \frac{E}{D \times \text{Availability}} \\ &= \frac{74.37}{15 \times 0.7} \\ &= \frac{74.37}{10.5} \\ &= 7.08 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{N \approx 8 \ \text{Developers}} $$ **Conclusion:** The project is a **Medium-sized, Major-effort** initiative requiring approximately **8 developers** for 15 months. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`** #### (a) Discuss the **CMMI 5-level maturity model** as a process categorization framework. Explain how each level influences project management practices. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Answer (a): **Introduction (1 Mark):** The Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI), developed by the Software Engineering Institute (SEI), categorizes organizations into 5 maturity levels based on the rigor of their software processes. The level dictates project management discipline. **Level 1 — Initial (1 Mark):** Processes are **chaotic and reactive**. Success depends on individual heroes. *Management Practice:* No standardized tracking; cost and schedule overruns are common. Project manager spends time firefighting rather than planning. **Level 2 — Managed (1 Mark):** Basic **project management** processes are established. Cost, schedule, and scope are tracked. *Management Practice:* Repeatable successes on similar projects. Project manager uses earned value analysis, requirements baselines, and configuration management. **Level 3 — Defined (1 Mark):** Processes are **standardized organization-wide**. Active use of organizational standard processes. *Management Practice:* Proactive risk identification, peer reviews, and process tailoring per project. The PM becomes a process-facilitator rather than a tracker. **Level 4 — Quantitatively Managed (1 Mark):** Process performance is **measured using statistical techniques**. Sub-process performance is controlled using data. *Management Practice:* Six Sigma-style metrics drive decisions. Predictive models (e.g., defect density forecasts) replace intuition. **Level 5 — Optimizing (1 Mark):** Continuous process improvement using **feedback from quantitative data**. *Management Practice:* Innovation and process improvement are institutionalized. The PM leads experiments (e.g., introducing TDD or pair programming) based on measured ROI. **Conclusion (1 Mark):** CMMI categorization directly influences tooling, estimation accuracy, audit overhead, and risk tolerance. KTU expects students to map project category → required CMMI level. --- #### (b) A bank intends to develop an **online loan approval system**. Categorize this project along the **6 dimensions** of categorization (domain, size, criticality, complexity, risk, and lifecycle). Justify each categorization. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Answer (b): | Dimension | Categorization | Justification | |---|---|---| | **1. Domain** (1 Mark) | **Business / Web Application Software** | The system automates a banking workflow (loan approval) and will be accessed via web/mobile. | | **2. Size** (1 Mark) | **Medium (estimated 30–50 KLOC)** | Online banking apps of this nature typically involve ~10–20 modules: customer KYC, credit scoring, document upload, payment gateway, notification service, admin dashboard. | | **3. Criticality** (1 Mark) | **Business-Critical** | Failure causes major business disruption (delayed loans, lost revenue, regulatory penalty) but no direct loss of life. Must comply with **RBI regulations** and **PCI-DSS**. | | **4. Complexity** (1 Mark) | **Moderate to Complex** | Integrations with credit bureaus, payment gateways, and core banking systems make it complex. Real-time scoring logic adds algorithmic depth. | | **5. Risk** (1 Mark) | **High (P × I ≈ 4 × 4 = 16)** | High probability of regulatory non-compliance (RBI audits); high impact (₹crore penalties + reputational damage). | | **6. Lifecycle** (2 Marks) | **Hybrid (Agile + V-Model verification tail)** | Front-end features are iterative (Agile), but regulatory compliance and security testing demand rigorous V-Model-style verification at the end. | **Conclusion:** This is a **Medium-sized, Business-Critical, Moderately Complex, High-Risk Web Application** best managed using a **Hybrid lifecycle** under **CMMI Level 3** discipline. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Single-dimension categorization loses 50% marks.** Students often say *"It is a web app"* and stop. Always cite *at least three dimensions* — domain + size + criticality. > 2. **Wrong lifecycle mapping.** Mapping a safety-critical avionics project to Agile is an instant 2-mark penalty. Map *Waterfall for stable*, *Agile for evolving*, *V-Model for verifiable*, *Spiral for risky*. > 3. **Numerical errors in COCOMO.** Forgetting the exponent $b$ (using plain multiplication) or mis-applying effort multipliers is the most common error. Always show the step. > 4. **Conflating Criticality with Risk.** Criticality = *consequence of failure* (static). Risk = *probability × impact* (dynamic). Examiners explicitly test this distinction. > 5. **Ignoring CMMI.** Even if the question does not ask for it, mentioning the *process maturity context* earns bonus marks. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Categorization"** - **Definition:** Categorization = classifying software/projects by domain, size, criticality, complexity, risk, and process maturity. - **6 Dimensions to memorize:** Domain, Size, Criticality, Complexity, Risk, Process (CMMI). - **Size Thresholds (COCOMO-aligned):** Trivial < 1, Small 1–10, Medium 10–100, Large 100–1000, Very Large > 1000 KLOC. - **Criticality Hierarchy:** Safety > Mission > Business > Business-Supportive > Non-Critical. - **Safety-Critical Standards:** DO-178C, IEC 62304, ISO 26262. - **Risk Formula:** $R = P \times I$, where $P, I \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$, giving $R \in [1, 25]$. - **CMMI Levels:** 1-Initial, 2-Managed, 3-Defined, 4-Quantitatively Managed, 5-Optimizing. - **COCOMO Organic Parameters:** $a = 1.4$, $b = 1.05$. - **Team Size Formula:** $N = \dfrac{E}{D \times \text{Availability}}$, with availability ∈ [0.6, 0.8]. - **Lifecycle Mapping:** - Stable requirements → **Waterfall** - Evolving requirements → **Agile** - High risk → **Spiral** - Safety-critical verification → **V-Model** - Mixed → **Hybrid** - **Golden Rule:** Always produce a *tuple* — e.g., `"Real-Time + Large + Safety-Critical + Complex + High-Risk → V-Model"`. - **KTU Hallmark:** A well-answered categorization question cites *≥ 3 dimensions*, *justifies each*, and *maps to a lifecycle model*.

topics.title: Project Charter
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Project Charter

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

A **Project Charter** is a document issued by the **sponsor** (or senior management) that **formally authorizes** the existence of a project and grants the **project manager** the authority to apply organizational resources to project activities. It serves as the input to the *Develop Project Management Plan* process and provides the project manager with the mandate to execute the project.

According to **PMBOK (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** terminology adopted by KTU, the Project Charter establishes a partnership between the requesting and performing organizations and links the project to the ongoing strategic execution of the organization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlight (Board Examiner Focus):**
> The Project Charter is the **output of the Develop Project Charter process** (a process performed **only once** or at predefined project milestones). It is created **before** the Project Management Plan. The Charter authorizes the project; the Plan defines *how* to execute it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you want to build a new house. Before any brick is laid, the **owner** writes a formal letter to the **contractor**. That letter states:

- *"Why"* the house is being built (a family of four needs a 3BHK).
- *"What"* it should contain (number of rooms, garden, garage).
- *"How much"* the owner is willing to spend (₹50 Lakhs).
- *"When"* it should be ready (next December).
- *"Who"* is the contractor and what authority they hold (can hire masons, buy materials).

This letter is the **Project Charter**. The detailed blue-print, materials list, daily schedule, and execution strategy is the **Project Management Plan**. Without the Charter, the contractor has **no official permission** to start work.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam answers, always mention that the Charter is **issued by the Sponsor**, not created by the Project Manager. The Project Manager is only **authorized** by the Charter.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Project Sponsor**: The person who provides financial resources and is the primary stakeholder.
- **Business Case**: The economic feasibility study that **precedes** and **justifies** the Charter.
- **Success Criteria**: Quantifiable standards used to measure project success.
- **Approval Requirements**: Define what, when, and by whom decisions require approval.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose and Strategic Role of the Project Charter

The Project Charter serves **four critical strategic purposes** in software project management:

1. **Formal Authorization** — Provides the project manager with the written authority to mobilize resources.
2. **Strategic Alignment** — Ensures the project is linked to the organization's business strategy.
3. **Stakeholder Alignment** — Establishes a shared understanding of project scope, objectives, and boundaries among key stakeholders.
4. **Risk & Constraint Acknowledgement** — Documents high-level risks, assumptions, and constraints before detailed planning.

## 2.2 Components of a Project Charter (PMBOK-Aligned)

A standard Project Charter comprises the following structured components:

| # | Component | Description |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Project Purpose / Business Case Justification** | The reason the project is being undertaken; business need. |
| 2 | **Measurable Objectives & Success Criteria** | Quantifiable goals (e.g., reduce response time by 30%). |
| 3 | **High-Level Requirements** | Major functional/non-functional needs from stakeholders. |
| 4 | **Project Description & Boundaries** | Scope summary; what is **included** and **excluded**. |
| 5 | **Risks & Assumptions** | High-level risks and assumptions documented at initiation. |
| 6 | **Summary Milestone Schedule** | Target dates for major deliverables. |
| 7 | **Summary Budget** | Approved cost estimate (often a range). |
| 8 | **Approval Requirements** | What decisions need authorization, by whom, and when. |
| 9 | **Assigned Project Manager & Authority Level** | PM's name, responsibility, and decision-making authority. |
| 10 | **Name & Authority of Sponsor** | The person authorizing the Charter. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

While the Project Charter is documentation-heavy, the following **PMBOK-aligned success metrics** are often used to define success criteria inside a Charter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost Performance Index (CPI)} &= \frac{EV}{AC} \\
\text{Schedule Performance Index (SPI)} &= \frac{EV}{PV} \\
\text{Cost Variance (CV)} &= EV - AC \\
\text{Schedule Variance (SV)} &= EV - PV
\end{aligned}
$$

| Metric | Symbol | Formula | Healthy Range |
|--------|--------|---------|---------------|
| Earned Value | $EV$ | $\% \text{ Complete} \times BAC$ | — |
| Planned Value | $PV$ | $\text{Planned work to date}$ | — |
| Actual Cost | $AC$ | $\text{Actual cost incurred}$ | — |
| Budget at Completion | $BAC$ | $\text{Total approved budget}$ | — |
| CPI | Ratio | $EV / AC$ | $\geq 1$ is good |
| SPI | Ratio | $EV / PV$ | $\geq 1$ is good |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter for the Charter:**
> The **success criteria** in a Charter are often expressed in terms of these EVM (Earned Value Management) thresholds. For example: *"Project is successful if final CPI $\geq$ 1.0 and SPI $\geq$ 1.0 at handover."*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real **software firms** (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Google, Microsoft), the Charter equivalent is often called a **Project Initiation Document (PID)** or **Statement of Work (SOW)**. It is used to:

- Secure board-level approval for capital expenditure.
- Trigger staffing and procurement.
- Define escalation paths for issues.
- Provide a reference document for audit and compliance (CMMI Level 2 maturity).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Process to Develop a Project Charter

The Charter is produced through the **Develop Project Charter Process**. Below is the exhaustive, KTU-board-approved procedure:

### **Step 1 — Identify the Business Need**
- Conduct meetings with the requesting party/sponsor.
- Document the business problem, opportunity, or regulatory compliance need.

### **Step 2 — Review Existing Documentation**
- Pull relevant inputs:
  - **Business Case**
  - **Agreements / Contracts** (SOW, MOUs)
  - **Organizational Process Assets (OPAs)**
  - **Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEFs)**

### **Step 3 — Conduct Expert Judgment Sessions**
- Consult SMEs (Subject Matter Experts), PMO, and senior management.
- Capture subjective insights on feasibility, technology, and constraints.

### **Step 4 — Use Facilitation Techniques**
- Apply techniques like:
  - **Brainstorming**
  - **Focus Groups**
  - **Conflict Resolution** (if stakeholders disagree)

### **Step 5 — Define Success Criteria Quantitatively**
- Set SMART objectives: **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound**.
- Example: *"Reduce API response time to **< 200 ms** by **Q4 2025** with a budget of **₹20 Lakhs**."*

### **Step 6 — Identify High-Level Risks, Assumptions & Constraints**
- **Assumptions**: e.g., "Cloud services will remain available 99.9% of the time."
- **Constraints**: e.g., "Must use existing legacy database."
- **Risks**: e.g., "Vendor may delay API delivery by 3 weeks."

### **Step 7 — Establish Milestone Schedule and Summary Budget**
- Estimate completion dates for major phases (Initiation, Planning, Execution, Closure).
- Assign a top-down budgetary envelope.

### **Step 8 — Formally Document the Charter**
- Compile all components into a single document following organizational templates.

### **Step 9 — Obtain Sponsor Sign-off and Authorization**
- The sponsor formally signs the Charter.
- Upon signature, the **project manager is authorized** to begin planning.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Inputs vs. Outputs vs. Tools of the Charter Process

| Category | Specific Elements |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Inputs** | Business Case, Agreements (SOW), EEFs, OPAs |
| **Tools & Techniques** | Expert Judgment, Facilitation Techniques, Analytical Techniques |
| **Outputs** | **Project Charter** (sole output) |

## 3.3 Numerical Illustration of Charter-Based Success Metrics

**Problem Statement:**
A software project has:
- Total Approved Budget ($BAC$) = **₹10,00,000**
- After 4 months, 50% of work is physically complete.
- Planned value at month 4 ($PV$) = **₹4,50,000**
- Actual cost incurred ($AC$) = **₹5,50,000**

**Find:** EV, CV, SV, CPI, SPI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Earned Value (EV)} &= \%\text{ Complete} \times BAC \\
&= 0.50 \times 10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost Variance (CV)} &= EV - AC \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000 \\
&= -50{,}000 \quad (\text{Negative} \Rightarrow \text{Over Budget})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Schedule Variance (SV)} &= EV - PV \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000 \\
&= +50{,}000 \quad (\text{Positive} \Rightarrow \text{Ahead of Schedule})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost Performance Index (CPI)} &= \frac{EV}{AC} \\
&= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} \\
&\approx 0.91 \quad (<1 \Rightarrow \text{Cost inefficient})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Schedule Performance Index (SPI)} &= \frac{EV}{PV} \\
&= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} \\
&\approx 1.11 \quad (>1 \Rightarrow \text{Ahead of schedule})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Board Exam Interpretation:** A CPI of 0.91 implies that for every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.91 worth of work is being achieved. This is a **trigger** for sponsor-level review under the Charter's approval requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Project Charter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram maps the structural composition of a Project Charter and its interactions with upstream/downstream project processes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Business Case] --> B[Develop Project Charter Process]
    A2[Agreements SOW] --> B
    A3[EEFs and OPAs] --> B
    B --> C1[Project Charter Document]
    C1 --> D1[Project Purpose Justification]
    C1 --> D2[Measurable Objectives]
    C1 --> D3[High Level Requirements]
    C1 --> D4[Project Description Boundaries]
    C1 --> D5[Risks Assumptions Constraints]
    C1 --> D6[Milestone Schedule Summary Budget]
    C1 --> D7[Approval Requirements]
    C1 --> D8[Project Manager Authority Level]
    C1 --> D9[Sponsor Name and Authority]
    C1 --> E[Sponsor Sign Off Authorization]
    E --> F[Develop Project Management Plan Next Process]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Charter to Closure

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS[Sponsor] -->|Issues Authorization| node1[Project Charter]
    node1 -->|Authorizes PM| node2[Project Manager]
    node2 -->|Creates| node3[Project Management Plan]
    node3 -->|Guides| node4[Project Execution]
    node4 -->|Monitors via| node5[EVM Metrics CPI SPI]
    node5 -->|Reports to| nodeS
```

## 4.3 Stakeholder Relationship Map for Charter

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph authorizationLayer [Authorization Layer]
        sponsor[Project Sponsor]
    end
    subgraph executionLayer [Execution Layer]
        pm[Project Manager]
        team[Project Team]
    end
    subgraph alignmentLayer [Alignment Layer]
        stakeholders[Key Stakeholders]
        sme[Subject Matter Experts]
    end
    sponsor -->|Signs Charter| pm
    pm -->|Plans and Directs| team
    sponsor -->|Aligns Expectations| stakeholders
    sme -->|Provides Inputs| pm
    stakeholders -->|Validates Scope| pm
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Project Charter. Who issues it?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A Project Charter is a document issued by the **sponsor** or senior management that **formally authorizes** the existence of a project. It grants the **project manager** the authority to apply organizational resources to project activities and defines high-level scope, objectives, risks, budget, milestones, and approval requirements. The Charter is the **output of the Develop Project Charter process** and serves as the input to planning.

*(Word limit: ~70 words. Add a real-time example to score full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. List any six components of a Project Charter.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
1. Project purpose / business case justification
2. Measurable objectives and success criteria
3. High-level requirements
4. Project description and boundaries
5. Summary milestone schedule and budget
6. Approval requirements, assigned PM authority, and sponsor name

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Standard:** KTU requires students to answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B in full**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **purpose** of a Project Charter in software project management. List and briefly describe **any seven components** of a Project Charter. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between a **Project Charter** and a **Project Management Plan**. Discuss the role of the **Project Sponsor** in authorizing the Charter. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Q.A(a)**

The **purpose** of a Project Charter is multi-fold:

1. **Formal Authorization** — It legally empowers the project manager to commit organizational resources.
2. **Strategic Alignment** — It links the project to business strategy defined in the Business Case.
3. **Stakeholder Alignment** — Creates early consensus among stakeholders on scope, success criteria, and boundaries.
4. **Risk Acknowledgement** — Captures high-level risks, assumptions, and constraints at initiation.

**Seven Components of a Project Charter:**

1. **Project Purpose / Justification** — Business need being addressed.
2. **Measurable Objectives & Success Criteria** — Quantifiable metrics (e.g., cost, time, quality thresholds).
3. **High-Level Requirements** — Summary of stakeholder needs.
4. **Project Description & Boundaries** — What is in scope and out of scope.
5. **Summary Milestone Schedule** — Major phase end-dates.
6. **Summary Budget** — Total approved funding envelope.
7. **Approval Requirements** — Define decision authorities.

### **Model Solution — Q.A(b)**

| Aspect | Project Charter | Project Management Plan |
|--------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Authorizes the project | Defines *how* to execute it |
| **Issuer** | Sponsor / Senior Management | Project Manager |
| **Created When** | Initiation phase | Planning phase |
| **Detail Level** | High-level summary | Detailed, exhaustive |
| **Scope** | Overall mandate | WBS, baselines, sub-plans |
| **Inputs to** | Develop Project Management Plan | Direct & Manage Project Work |

**Role of Project Sponsor:**

- The Sponsor **owns** the Business Case and provides the financial resources.
- The Sponsor **drafts, validates, and signs** the Project Charter.
- The Sponsor **authorizes** the PM to begin work and acts as the **escalation point** for major risks.
- The Sponsor ensures the project remains aligned with organizational strategy throughout its lifecycle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Past Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Develop Project Charter process** in detail. List its inputs, tools & techniques, and outputs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A software project has $BAC = 10,00,000$. At month 4, 50% work is complete. $PV = 4,50,000$ and $AC = 5,50,000$. Calculate **EV, CV, SV, CPI, SPI** and **interpret** the project's health as a measure of success criteria from the Charter. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Q.B(a)**

**Develop Project Charter Process:**

This is the **first process** in the **Initiation Process Group**. It is performed once or at predefined milestones to formally authorize a project.

**Inputs:**
- Business Case (justifies the project economically)
- Agreements / SOW (with external parties)
- Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEFs)
- Organizational Process Assets (OPAs)

**Tools & Techniques:**
- Expert Judgment
- Facilitation Techniques (brainstorming, conflict resolution)
- Analytical Techniques (SWOT, cost-benefit)

**Outputs:**
- **Project Charter** (sole output)

### **Model Solution — Q.B(b)**

Given: $BAC = 10,00,000$, % Complete = 50%, $PV = 4,50,000$, $AC = 5,50,000$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EV &= 0.50 \times 10,00,000 = 5,00,000 \\
CV &= EV - AC = 5,00,000 - 5,50,000 = -50,000 \quad (\text{Over Budget}) \\
SV &= EV - PV = 5,00,000 - 4,50,000 = +50,000 \quad (\text{Ahead of Schedule}) \\
CPI &= \frac{5,00,000}{5,50,000} \approx 0.91 \quad (< 1, \text{Cost inefficient}) \\
SPI &= \frac{5,00,000}{4,50,000} \approx 1.11 \quad (> 1, \text{Schedule efficient})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Charter-Based Interpretation:**
If the Charter's success criterion states "Final CPI $\geq 1.0$ and SPI $\geq 1.0$", then **the cost criterion is currently failing**. The PM must initiate corrective action under the **Approval Requirements clause** of the Charter. SPI being >1 may only reflect rushed early-stage work — the trend must be monitored.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT say** the Project Manager creates the Charter. It is **issued by the Sponsor** — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse** Charter with Project Management Plan. They are entirely different documents in different process groups.
> 3. **Always cite PMBOK terminology** — "Initiation Process Group", "Develop Project Charter", "Sponsor" — examiners reward correct terminology with 1–2 bonus marks.
> 4. In numericals, **show units (₹) and formulas** clearly; missing units cost marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Project Charter** is a sponsor-issued authorization document, NOT a project plan.
- The Charter is the **output of the Develop Project Charter Process** (Initiation Process Group).
- It contains: purpose, objectives, requirements, boundaries, risks, milestones, budget, approval requirements, PM authority, and sponsor authority.
- It is created **before** the Project Management Plan.
- The **Project Manager is authorized** by the Charter, not the author of it.
- **EVM metrics** (CPI, SPI) derived from Charter success criteria are board-level tracking measures.
- It establishes **strategic alignment, stakeholder agreement, and risk visibility**.
- A **Business Case precedes** a Project Charter; a **Project Management Plan follows** it.
- The Charter authorizes the use of organizational resources — without it, no expenditure or staffing can be formally committed.
- Remember the magic formula:

$$
\text{Charter} \Rightarrow \text{Authorization} \Rightarrow \text{Planning} \Rightarrow \text{Execution} \Rightarrow \text{Monitoring} \Rightarrow \text{Closure}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stakeholders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stakeholders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stakeholder (PMBOK / IEEE Standard Definition):**
> A **stakeholder** is an individual, group, or organization that may affect, be affected by, or perceive itself to be affected by a decision, activity, or outcome of a software project. Stakeholders have interests, rights, or ownership in the project, its deliverables, or its environment, and their active or passive involvement can influence project success or failure.

In the context of **Software Project Management (PEITT633)**, stakeholders are broadly classified under the **Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK) – 7th Edition** framework as one of the **six core performance domains**: *Stakeholder Performance Domain*.

### 1.2 Expanded Operational Definition

A stakeholder in software engineering is **any entity** (human or institutional) that:
1. **Provides inputs** (requirements, resources, funding) to the project.
2. **Receives outputs** (deliverables, reports, deployed software) from the project.
3. **Imposes constraints** (regulatory, legal, technical, ethical) on the project.
4. **Has expectations** of value, benefit, or behaviour from the project.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly requires students to distinguish between **internal vs. external stakeholders**, **direct vs. indirect stakeholders**, and to apply the **Power/Interest Grid (Mendelow's Matrix)** for stakeholder prioritization. This is a high-frequency ESE (End Semester Examination) topic.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### 🎬 The "Apartment Building Construction" Analogy

Imagine you are building a **20-floor apartment complex**. You are the builder (Project Manager), but you are *never alone* in this endeavour:

| Real-World Entity | Software Project Equivalent | Role in Project |
|---|---|---|
| The **buyer** who books flat #1403 | The **Client / Customer** | Pays for the deliverable |
| The **architect** who draws the blueprint | The **Business Analyst / System Architect** | Defines requirements & design |
| The **masons & electricians** | The **Developers / Testers** | Build the product |
| The **municipality** that issues permits | The **Regulatory / Legal body** | Imposes compliance rules |
| The **neighbours** complaining about noise | The **End Users / Society** | Affected by the outcome |
| The **bank** that gave the loan | The **Sponsor / Investor** | Funds the project |
| The **supplier** of cement & steel | The **Vendor / Subcontractor** | Provides external resources |

Every single one of them can **make or break** your building. The same is true for software projects. A **stakeholder** is simply *"anyone who has a stake (interest) in your project's outcome."*

### 🍕 Even Simpler Analogy — "Ordering a Pizza"

When you order a pizza, the **stakeholders** are:
- **You** (the customer — wants hot, tasty pizza)
- **The chef** (must cook it well)
- **The delivery boy** (must deliver on time)
- **The pizza shop owner** (wants profit)
- **Your family** (will also eat it — indirect stakeholders)
- **The food safety inspector** (ensures hygiene compliance)

If the delivery boy doesn't show up, the chef's effort is wasted. **Managing the delivery boy is as critical as managing the chef.** This is *stakeholder management*.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Universal Classifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Universal Stakeholder Classification Schemes (KTU Board-Expected):**
> 1. **Internal vs. External Stakeholders**
> 2. **Primary (Direct) vs. Secondary (Indirect) Stakeholders**
> 3. **Positive vs. Negative Stakeholders**
>
> The classification **power × interest** is a **quantitative analytical tool** and is always asked in ESE.

---

## 1.5 KTU Course Outcome Mapping

| KTU CO ID | Course Outcome Statement | Topic Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | *Demonstrate the principles of software project management* | Stakeholder identification & classification directly supports CO1 |
| **CO2** | *Apply project planning techniques for software projects* | Stakeholder management is a sub-process of project planning |

| KTU Bloom's Level | Cognitive Demand |
|---|---|
| **L1 – Remember** | Define stakeholders, list types |
| **L2 – Understand** | Distinguish internal/external, primary/secondary |
| **L3 – Apply** | Apply Power/Interest Grid to a scenario |
| **L4 – Analyze** | Prioritize stakeholders for a given case study |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Matrix

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Stakeholders

Stakeholder classification is the **first analytical step** in software project management. Without classification, prioritization becomes impossible. Below is the exhaustive taxonomy as per KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 2.1.1 By Origin / Location

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Stakeholders** operate *within* the organizational boundary of the performing organization. They are directly involved in the project execution.
> **External Stakeholders** operate *outside* the performing organization but are affected by or can affect the project.

| Internal Stakeholders | External Stakeholders |
|---|---|
| Project Manager | Customer / Client |
| Project Team Members (Developers, Testers) | End Users |
| Functional Managers | Vendors / Suppliers |
| Business Analysts | Regulatory Bodies (e.g., RBI, SEBI, FDA) |
| Product Owner (in Agile) | Competitors |
| Internal Sponsors / Senior Management | Shareholders / Investors |
| Quality Assurance Team | Media / Press |
| HR & Admin Support | Public / Society at large |
| Configuration Management Team | Subcontractors |
| Documentation Team | Legal & Compliance Authorities |

---

### 2.1.2 By Directness of Impact

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary (Direct) Stakeholders** are *engaged in the economic transaction* of the project — they directly provide or receive value.
> **Secondary (Indirect) Stakeholders** are *affected by the project's outcomes* but are not directly involved in the exchange.

| Primary Stakeholders | Secondary Stakeholders |
|---|---|
| Project Sponsor | Government & Regulators |
| Project Team | Society & Community |
| Customer / Client | Media & Public |
| End Users | Environmental groups |
| Suppliers / Vendors | Future generations (for sustainability) |
| Investors / Shareholders | Industry associations |
| Project Manager | Trade unions |
| Functional Managers | Academic & research bodies |

---

### 2.1.3 By Attitude / Stance

> [!NOTE]
> **Positive Stakeholders** benefit from the project's success and therefore *support* it.
> **Negative Stakeholders** perceive the project as a threat to their interests and therefore *resist* it.

| Positive Stakeholders | Negative Stakeholders |
|---|---|
| Sponsor (wants ROI) | Competing vendor (loses market share) |
| Project team (wants success) | Employees fearing redundancy due to automation |
| Customer (wants product) | Regulatory body (cites non-compliance) |
| End users (want useful software) | Trade union (if jobs are threatened) |
| Shareholders (want profits) | Lobby groups (e.g., privacy advocates) |

---

## 2.2 Stakeholder Identification Techniques

Identifying stakeholders is a **continuous process** — it must be done at the *initiation phase* and updated throughout the project lifecycle.

| Technique | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Brainstorming** | Cross-functional team generates a list of all potential stakeholders | Initiation Phase |
| **Expert Judgment** | Consultation with subject matter experts (SMEs) | Initiation Phase |
| **Stakeholder Analysis** | Systematic gathering and analysis of stakeholder information | Planning Phase |
| **Stakeholder Register** | A document listing all identified stakeholders, their roles, and contact details | Initiation / Planning |
| **Questionnaires & Surveys** | Distributed to a wide audience to identify less obvious stakeholders | Initiation Phase |
| **Document Analysis** | Reviewing contracts, agreements, and previous project documents | Planning Phase |
| **Assumption & Constraint Analysis** | Identifying stakeholders from documented assumptions and constraints | Planning Phase |
| **Delivery & Industry Knowledge** | Leveraging PMBOK, industry standards, and benchmarking | Initiation Phase |

---

## 2.3 The Power / Interest Grid (Mendelow's Matrix) — *The Most Critical KTU Topic*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mendelow's Power/Interest Grid** is a **2×2 analytical matrix** used to categorize stakeholders based on two attributes:
> 1. **Power** — The ability of the stakeholder to influence the project or organization.
> 2. **Interest** — The level of concern the stakeholder has about the project's outcome.

The matrix divides stakeholders into **four strategic quadrants**, each requiring a different engagement strategy.

### KTU High-Yield Analytical Matrix

| Quadrant | Power | Interest | Strategy | Engagement Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Q1 — Manage Closely** | High | High | **Active Management**: Engage fully, satisfy their demands, build strong relationships | **High Priority** — frequent communication, full involvement in decisions |
| **Q2 — Keep Satisfied** | High | Low | **Strategic Monitoring**: Keep them informed enough; do not over-engage; address concerns proactively | **Medium Priority** — provide summarized reports, address issues promptly |
| **Q3 — Keep Informed** | Low | High | **Informational Engagement**: Provide regular updates; they have interest but limited power | **Medium Priority** — newsletters, user group meetings, FAQs |
| **Q4 — Monitor (Minimum Effort)** | Low | Low | **Minimal Effort**: Monitor with least resource expenditure | **Low Priority** — general broadcast communication only |

### 2.3.1 Visual Mapping of Stakeholders

| Software Project Example | Power | Interest | Quadrant |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Project Sponsor (CXO)** | High | High | Q1 — Manage Closely |
| **Senior Management** | High | Low | Q2 — Keep Satisfied |
| **End Users** | Low | High | Q3 — Keep Informed |
| **General Public** | Low | Low | Q4 — Monitor |
| **Regulatory Body (e.g., RBI for banking software)** | High | High | Q1 — Manage Closely |
| **Project Team Members** | Low | High | Q3 — Keep Informed |
| **External Auditor** | High | Low | Q2 — Keep Satisfied |

---

## 2.4 The PMI / PMBOK Stakeholder Cube (Three-Dimensional View)

> [!NOTE]
> PMBOK 6th Edition introduced the **Stakeholder Cube / Classification Model**, which classifies stakeholders along three dimensions:
> 1. **Power / Authority** (high or low)
> 2. **Legitimacy** (legitimate or illegitimate)
> 3. **Urgency** (urgent or not urgent)
>
> When all three intersect, the stakeholder is termed a **"Definitive Stakeholder"** and must be given the highest priority.

### The Three-Attribute Stakeholder Cube Table

| Attribute | Definition | Example in Software Project |
|---|---|---|
| **Power** | Ability to impose their will | Project Sponsor can cancel the project |
| **Legitimacy** | Right or claim to be involved | Customer who has signed the contract |
| **Urgency** | Need for immediate attention | Production server crash — operations team |

| Stakeholder Type | Power | Legitimacy | Urgency | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Definitive** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **Highest priority** — full engagement |
| **Dependent** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | Need support of powerful stakeholder |
| **Dormant** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Monitor; may become active |
| **Dangerous** | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Potentially coercive; manage carefully |
| **Non-stakeholder** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Ignore |

---

## 2.5 Stakeholder Management Process (PMBOK Process Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **PMBOK 6th Edition**, Stakeholder Management consists of **four processes** (which are examinable):

| # | Process | Process Group | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identify Stakeholders** | Initiating | Stakeholder Register |
| 2 | **Plan Stakeholder Management** | Planning | Stakeholder Management Plan |
| 3 | **Manage Stakeholder Engagement** | Executing | Issue Logs, Change Requests |
| 4 | **Monitor Stakeholder Engagement** | Monitoring & Controlling | Work Performance Information |

---

## 2.6 Stakeholder Register — The Master Document

> [!NOTE]
> A **Stakeholder Register** is a project document that lists all identified stakeholders, along with their:
> - Identification information (name, role, organization)
> - Assessment information (requirements, expectations, influence)
> - Classification (internal/external, impact, phase of interest)

| Field | Example Entry |
|---|---|
| **Stakeholder Name** | Mr. Arun Krishnan |
| **Role** | Chief Technology Officer (CTO) |
| **Organization** | ClientCo Ltd. |
| **Contact Info** | arun@clientco.com, +91-9876543210 |
| **Expectations** | On-time delivery, 99.9% uptime, GDPR compliance |
| **Influence** | High (can cancel the project) |
| **Interest** | High (directly accountable to board) |
| **Phase of Interest** | All phases |
| **Classification** | Internal, Primary, Positive, High Power–High Interest |

---

## 2.7 Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix tracks the **gap between current and desired engagement levels** for each stakeholder.

| Engagement Level | Description |
|---|---|
| **Unaware** | Does not know about the project and its impacts |
| **Resistant** | Aware of the project and resistant to change |
| **Neutral** | Aware of the project but neither supportive nor resistant |
| **Supportive** | Aware of the project and supportive of change |
| **Leading** | Actively engaged in ensuring project success |

### The Engagement Gap Formula (Conceptual)

$$
\Delta E = E_{desired} - E_{current}
$$

Where:
- $E_{desired}$ = Desired engagement level (e.g., *Leading*)
- $E_{current}$ = Current engagement level (e.g., *Neutral*)
- $\Delta E$ = Engagement gap (the *shortfall* to be closed by management actions)

> **Interpretation:** A positive $\Delta E$ indicates a need for *proactive engagement actions*; a zero or negative value indicates *maintenance strategies* are sufficient.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Stakeholder Management Application |
|---|---|
| **Banking Software (e.g., Core Banking Systems)** | Managing RBI, SEBI, customers, internal auditors, cyber-security teams |
| **Healthcare Software (e.g., HIS, EHR)** | Managing HIPAA compliance, doctors, patients, insurance providers |
| **E-Commerce (e.g., Amazon, Flipkart)** | Managing vendors (sellers), customers, logistics partners, payment gateways |
| **Defence / Aerospace Software** | Managing MoD, DRDO, security agencies, stringent compliance bodies |
| **EdTech Platforms (e.g., Coursera, Byju's)** | Managing students, teachers, parents, content creators, regulators |
| **Government e-Governance (e.g., DigiLocker, UMANG)** | Managing citizens, ministries, auditors, RTI activists, media |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Case Study Resolution

> [!NOTE]
> Since *Stakeholders* is a **management / conceptual topic** (not a numerical engineering subject), "derivations" here refer to **step-by-step analytical procedures** for classifying and prioritizing stakeholders in a given scenario. The mathematical representations are *conceptual* but formally expressed.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Procedure: Applying the Power/Interest Grid to a Software Project Scenario

### 📘 Given Case Study (KTU 2024 Style ESE Question)

> *"ABC Software Pvt. Ltd. is developing a new mobile banking application for a nationalized bank. The project has the following entities:*
> 1. *Project Sponsor — General Manager of IT, Bank*
> 2. *Project Manager — Senior Manager, ABC Software*
> 3. *Development Team — 8 engineers at ABC Software*
> 4. *End Users — Bank's 5 million retail customers*
> 5. *RBI — Banking regulator*
> 6. *External Auditor — Statutory auditor of the bank*
> 7. *Vendor — Third-party SMS gateway provider*
> 8. *News Media — Financial journalists*
>
> *Classify these stakeholders using the Power/Interest Grid and recommend engagement strategies."*

### Step-by-Step Analytical Resolution

#### **Step 1: Identify and Tabulate All Stakeholders**

| ID | Stakeholder | Type (Internal/External) | Power (H/L) | Interest (H/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Project Sponsor (GM-IT) | External | **High** | **High** |
| S2 | Project Manager (Sr. Manager) | Internal | High | High |
| S3 | Development Team | Internal | Low | **High** |
| S4 | End Users (5M customers) | External | Low | **High** |
| S5 | RBI (Regulator) | External | **High** | Low |
| S6 | External Auditor | External | **High** | Low |
| S7 | SMS Gateway Vendor | External | Low | **High** |
| S8 | News Media | External | Low | Low |

#### **Step 2: Justify Power and Interest Ratings**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU Board expects **explicit justification** for every rating. Simply stating "High" without reasoning will lose marks.

**Justification Table:**

| Stakeholder | Power Justification | Interest Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Project Sponsor | Can cancel project, controls budget (₹50 Cr) | Direct ROI accountability to bank board |
| Project Manager | Controls day-to-day delivery, schedules resources | Career and professional reputation at stake |
| Dev Team | Cannot cancel project, but can deliver quality | Direct execution responsibility, skills growth |
| End Users | Individually low power, but collective voice matters | Will use the app daily — high personal stake |
| RBI | Legal authority — can revoke banking license | Statutory interest in compliance; not in this specific app's UX |
| External Auditor | Can flag findings in audit report | Interest only in regulatory compliance, not features |
| SMS Vendor | Low individual power, but critical for OTP delivery | Revenue dependency on transaction volume |
| News Media | Cannot directly influence; influences public perception | General interest in banking sector news, not this app |

#### **Step 3: Map Each Stakeholder to the Correct Quadrant**

$$
\text{Quadrant}(S_i) = f(\text{Power}(S_i), \text{Interest}(S_i))
$$

Formally, the mapping function is:

$$
Q(S_i) = \begin{cases} \text{Q1 (Manage Closely)} & \text{if } P(S_i) = H \text{ and } I(S_i) = H \\ \text{Q2 (Keep Satisfied)} & \text{if } P(S_i) = H \text{ and } I(S_i) = L \\ \text{Q3 (Keep Informed)} & \text{if } P(S_i) = L \text{ and } I(S_i) = H \\ \text{Q4 (Monitor)} & \text{if } P(S_i) = L \text{ and } I(S_i) = L \end{cases}
$$

Applying this rule:

| Stakeholder | Power | Interest | Quadrant |
|---|---|---|---|
| Project Sponsor | H | H | **Q1 — Manage Closely** |
| Project Manager | H | H | **Q1 — Manage Closely** |
| Dev Team | L | H | **Q3 — Keep Informed** |
| End Users | L | H | **Q3 — Keep Informed** |
| RBI | H | L | **Q2 — Keep Satisfied** |
| External Auditor | H | L | **Q2 — Keep Satisfied** |
| SMS Vendor | L | H | **Q3 — Keep Informed** |
| News Media | L | L | **Q4 — Monitor** |

#### **Step 4: Define Engagement Strategy for Each Quadrant**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners award full marks only if the **strategy is concrete and project-specific** — not generic textbook copy-paste.

| Quadrant | Stakeholders | Engagement Strategy (Project-Specific) |
|---|---|---|
| **Q1 — Manage Closely** | Sponsor, PM | Weekly steering committee meetings, monthly executive reviews, WhatsApp group for instant escalations, co-located war-room for critical milestones |
| **Q2 — Keep Satisfied** | RBI, External Auditor | Quarterly compliance reports, pre-emptive data security audits, submission of all RBI-mandated artefacts (DR plans, BCP) before deadlines |
| **Q3 — Keep Informed** | Dev Team, End Users, SMS Vendor | Daily stand-ups (Dev Team), monthly UAT workshops with sample users, SLAs monitored via real-time dashboards (Vendor) |
| **Q4 — Monitor** | News Media | Press release on app launch, no active engagement, monitor social media sentiment |

#### **Step 5: Quantify Engagement Priority Score (Optional Advanced Step)**

A **weighted priority score** can be computed as:

$$
P_{score}(S_i) = w_1 \cdot P(S_i) + w_2 \cdot I(S_i) + w_3 \cdot L(S_i) + w_4 \cdot U(S_i)
$$

Where:
- $P(S_i)$ = Power level (0 = Low, 1 = High)
- $I(S_i)$ = Interest level (0 = Low, 1 = High)
- $L(S_i)$ = Legitimacy (0/1)
- $U(S_i)$ = Urgency (0/1)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Weights summing to 1 (typically 0.3, 0.3, 0.2, 0.2)

> **Example:** For Project Sponsor with $P=1, I=1, L=1, U=0.5$ and equal weights:
>
> $$
> P_{score} = (0.25)(1) + (0.25)(1) + (0.25)(1) + (0.25)(0.5) = 0.875
> $$
>
> For News Media with $P=0, I=0, L=0, U=0$:
>
> $$
> P_{score} = (0.25)(0) + (0.25)(0) + (0.25)(0) + (0.25)(0) = 0.0
> $$

This confirms: **Sponsor is the highest priority stakeholder** for engagement resources.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Procedure: Resolving Stakeholder Conflict

### 📘 Scenario

> *"The Project Manager wants to use MongoDB (NoSQL) for the new product. The Client demands Oracle (RDBMS) for compliance. How should this conflict be resolved?"*

### Step-by-Step Resolution

1. **Identify both stakeholders** — PM (Q1, internal) and Client (Q1, external) — both in the high-power, high-interest quadrant.
2. **Document the conflict** in the **Issue Log** with full context.
3. **Gather data** — research both options; understand the *real* reason for the client's preference (often legacy integration, not technology itself).
4. **Convene a joint workshop** — present trade-off analysis:
   - MongoDB: Faster development, flexible schema, lower cost.
   - Oracle: Mature, compliance-friendly, integrates with client's existing systems.
5. **Propose a hybrid solution** — MongoDB for analytics layer, Oracle for transactional layer.
6. **Escalate to sponsor** if no agreement is reached within the agreed timeframe.
7. **Document resolution** in the **Stakeholder Management Plan** and **Decision Log**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key principle: **High-power stakeholders must always be engaged actively.** Never ignore them.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Procedure: Building a Stakeholder Register

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | List all potential stakeholders from all sources | Initial stakeholder list |
| **2** | Classify by type (Internal/External, Primary/Secondary) | Classification matrix |
| **3** | Assess power, interest, legitimacy, urgency | Assessment matrix |
| **4** | Map to Power/Interest Grid | Quadrant assignment |
| **5** | Define engagement strategy per quadrant | Engagement plan |
| **6** | Document in Stakeholder Register | Final Stakeholder Register |
| **7** | Review & update at every phase gate | Updated register |

> **Frequency of Update (KTU Expected Answer):** The Stakeholder Register must be reviewed at:
> - **Initiation Phase** (initial creation)
> - **Major Phase Gates** (at every milestone)
> - **When significant changes occur** (scope change, new regulation, team turnover)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Stakeholder Classification Master Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STAKEHOLDERS OF A SOFTWARE PROJECT]:::root

    A --> B[BY ORIGIN]
    A --> C[BY DIRECTNESS]
    A --> D[BY ATTITUDE]
    A --> E[BY POWER INTEREST GRID]

    B --> B1[INTERNAL]
    B --> B2[EXTERNAL]

    B1 --> B1A[Project Manager]
    B1 --> B1B[Dev Team]
    B1 --> B1C[QA Team]
    B1 --> B1D[Sponsor]
    B1 --> B1E[Functional Mgr]

    B2 --> B2A[Customer]
    B2 --> B2B[End User]
    B2 --> B2C[Vendor]
    B2 --> B2D[Regulator]
    B2 --> B2E[Media]

    C --> C1[PRIMARY DIRECT]
    C --> C2[SECONDARY INDIRECT]

    C1 --> C1A[Sponsor]
    C1 --> C1B[Team]
    C1 --> C1C[Client]
    C1 --> C1D[Users]

    C2 --> C2A[Government]
    C2 --> C2B[Society]
    C2 --> C2C[Media]
    C2 --> C2D[Environment Groups]

    D --> D1[POSITIVE]
    D --> D2[NEGATIVE]

    D1 --> D1A[Sponsor wants ROI]
    D1 --> D1B[Team wants success]
    D1 --> D1C[Users want product]

    D2 --> D2A[Competitor resists]
    D2 --> D2B[Regulator cites non compliance]
    D2 --> D2C[Union fears job loss]

    E --> E1[Q1 HIGH HIGH MANAGE CLOSELY]

    classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef primary fill:#16213e,stroke:#0f3460,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef secondary fill:#0f3460,stroke:#533483,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef quadrant fill:#533483,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Power/Interest Grid (Mendelow's Matrix) — 2×2 Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PG[POWER INTEREST GRID - MENDELOW MATRIX]
        direction LR

        subgraph HIGH_POW[High Power]
            direction TB
            Q1[Q1: MANAGE CLOSELY<br/>High Power + High Interest<br/>Sponsor, PM, Regulator]:::q1
            Q2[Q2: KEEP SATISFIED<br/>High Power + Low Interest<br/>Senior Mgmt, Auditor]:::q2
        end

        subgraph LOW_POW[Low Power]
            direction TB
            Q3[Q3: KEEP INFORMED<br/>Low Power + High Interest<br/>End Users, Dev Team]:::q3
            Q4[Q4: MONITOR<br/>Low Power + Low Interest<br/>Media, General Public]:::q4
        end
    end

    HIGH_INT[High Interest] -.-> Q1
    HIGH_INT -.-> Q3
    LOW_INT[Low Interest] -.-> Q2
    LOW_INT -.-> Q4

    HIGH_POW -.-> Q1
    HIGH_POW -.-> Q2
    LOW_POW -.-> Q3
    LOW_POW -.-> Q4

    classDef q1 fill:#e94560,stroke:#1a1a2e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef q2 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#1a1a2e,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef q3 fill:#3498db,stroke:#1a1a2e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef q4 fill:#95a5a6,stroke:#1a1a2e,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: PMBOK Stakeholder Management Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1([PROJECT INITIATION]):::phase
    P1 --> PR1[1. IDENTIFY STAKEHOLDERS]:::proc
    PR1 --> O1[STAKEHOLDER REGISTER]:::output
    O1 --> PR2[2. PLAN STAKEHOLDER MANAGEMENT]:::proc
    PR2 --> O2[STAKEHOLDER MANAGEMENT PLAN]:::output
    O2 --> PR3[3. MANAGE STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT]:::proc
    PR3 --> O3[ISSUE LOG, CHANGE REQUESTS]:::output
    O3 --> PR4[4. MONITOR STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT]:::proc
    PR4 --> O4[WORK PERFORMANCE INFORMATION]:::output
    O4 --> DEC{Engagement Gap?}:::decision
    DEC -->|Yes| PR3
    DEC -->|No| CLOSE([PROJECT CLOSURE]):::phase

    classDef phase fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Stakeholder Engagement Level Progression

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[1. UNAWARE<br/>Does not know project exists]:::l1
    L2[2. RESISTANT<br/>Aware and opposes]:::l2
    L3[3. NEUTRAL<br/>Aware but passive]:::l3
    L4[4. SUPPORTIVE<br/>Aware and supportive]:::l4
    L5[5. LEADING<br/>Actively champions project]:::l5

    L1 -->|Communication| L2
    L2 -->|Negotiation| L3
    L3 -->|Training| L4
    L4 -->|Recognition| L5

    L5 -.->|Slip back if neglected| L3
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STAKEHOLDER ATTRIBUTES]:::root

    A --> P[POWER<br/>Ability to influence]:::attr
    A --> L[LEGITIMACY<br/>Right to be involved]:::attr
    A --> U[URGENCY<br/>Need for immediate action]:::attr

    P --> DS[DEFINITIVE STAKEHOLDER<br/>Power + Legitimacy + Urgency]:::def

    U --> DS

    U --> DEP

    DS --> ACTION1[High Priority: Full Engagement]:::act
```

```mermaid
graph TD

    S1 --> ST1[Step 1: BRAINSTORM all potential stakeholders]:::step
```
