#### Model Answer for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The Benefits Management Life Cycle is a continuous four-stage framework that governs how benefits are identified, planned, realized, and sustained throughout and beyond the project.

**2. Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STAGE 1 IDENTIFY Benefits Register"] --> B["STAGE 2 PLAN BMP and KPI"]
    B --> C["STAGE 3 REALIZE Tracking and Reviews"]
    C --> D["STAGE 4 SUSTAIN BAU and Lessons"]
    D --> A
```

**3. Stage-wise documents (3 marks):**

| Stage | Key Activity | Document Output |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 — Identify | Stakeholder elicitation | Benefits Register (initial) |
| 2 — Plan | Quantify, assign owner | Benefits Management Plan |
| 3 — Realize | Monitor & review | Benefits Tracking Table, Realization Report |
| 4 — Sustain | Embed in BAU | Post-Implementation Review, Lessons Learned |

---

#### Model Answer for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Discount factors (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_{1} &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^{1}} = 0.8929 \\
DF_{2} &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^{2}} = 0.7972 \\
DF_{3} &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^{3}} = 0.7118
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Present Values (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{1} &= 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929 = 10{,}71{,}480 \\
PV_{2} &= 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = 14{,}34{,}960 \\
PV_{3} &= 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118 = 17{,}79{,}500 \\
TPVB  &= 10{,}71{,}480 + 14{,}34{,}960 + 17{,}79{,}500 = 42{,}85{,}940
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — NPV (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= TPVB - Investment \\
    &= 42{,}85{,}940 - 50{,}00{,}000 \\
    &= -7{,}14{,}060
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — ROI (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Net\ Benefit &= (12 + 18 + 25) \times 10^{5} - 50 \times 10^{5} = 55{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \\
ROI &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\ \% = 10\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — CBR (1 mark):**
$$
CBR = \frac{42{,}85{,}940}{50{,}00{,}000} = 0.8572
$$

**Step 6 — Decision (0.5 mark):**
Since NPV is **negative** and CBR < 1, the project should **NOT be approved** on pure financial grounds. Either the discount rate must be lower, or the benefits underestimated (intangibles should be added).

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PEITT633]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Benefits Management Plan (BMP)** in detail. Discuss its key sections and how it differs from the Project Management Plan. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **key challenges** of implementing Benefits Management in software projects and suggest **mitigation strategies** for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The Benefits Management Plan is a formal document that defines how and when the benefits of a project will be delivered, measured, and sustained.

**2. Key Sections of a BMP (4 marks):**

| Section | Content |
|---------|---------|
| Benefits Definition | Description of each benefit (tangible/intangible) |
| Benefits Owners | Person accountable per benefit |
| KPIs & Measurement | Metric, baseline, target, data source |
| Timeline | When each benefit is expected to materialize |
| Reporting | Frequency, format, audience of reviews |
| Risks & Assumptions | Benefit-level risks (e.g., adoption) |
| Sustainment Plan | How benefits continue post-go-live |

**3. BMP vs PMP — Difference (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Benefits Management Plan | Project Management Plan |
|--------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| Focus | Value realization | Scope, time, cost, quality |
| Owner | Business / Sponsor | Project Manager |
| Horizon | Continues post-project | Ends at project closure |
| Metrics | Business KPIs (₹, %, NPS) | Project KPIs (SPI, CPI) |
| Update Cadence | Periodic post-go-live | During project execution |

---

#### Model Answer for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Challenges and Mitigation Table (1 mark per row = 7 rows):**

| # | Challenge | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Benefits identified but not quantified | Apply NPV / ROI / CBR formulas during planning |
| 2 | No single owner for each benefit | RACI matrix with one Benefit Owner per benefit |
| 3 | Benefits realized but not measured | Embed KPI dashboards in operational reporting |
| 4 | Intangible benefits ignored | Capture via surveys, NPS, qualitative scoring |
| 5 | Business environment changes mid-project | Trigger re-baselining reviews quarterly |
| 6 | Ownership dispute between IT and Business | Sign-off of BMP by joint steering committee |
| 7 | Benefits not sustained post-go-live | Define BAU process owners in BMP sustainment section |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Alert — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing Output with Benefit:** Students frequently state *“the CRM software is the benefit”* — it is an *output*, not a benefit. The benefit is e.g., *“30 % higher customer retention.”* (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Skipping Discounting in NPV:** Forgetting the discount factor is the most common error in numericals — **(Lose up to 2 marks).**
> 3. **Omitting the Benefit Owner:** A BMP without explicit ownership is incomplete per KTU rubric — **(Lose 1 mark).**
> 4. **Mixing PMP and BMP:** Writing Project Management Plan content inside Benefits Management Plan — **(Lose 1–2 marks).**
> 5. **No Sustainment Plan:** KTU awards explicit marks for the post-go-live sustainment section.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Benefits Management = **identifying + planning + realizing + sustaining** value from a software project.
- ✅ Output → Outcome → Impact is the **causal chain** you must remember verbatim.
- ✅ The **Benefit Owner** is a *business-side* role — never the developer or tester.
- ✅ **Benefits Management Plan (BMP)** is a sub-plan of the Project Management Plan, not a replacement.
- ✅ **Tangible** benefits = monetary; **Intangible** = perceptual (brand, morale, satisfaction).
- ✅ **Dis-benefits** (negative consequences) must also be tracked.
- ✅ **NPV** uses *discounted* cash flows; **ROI** uses *undiscounted* net benefit.
- ✅ **CBR > 1** and **NPV > 0** are the twin gates for financial approval.
- ✅ Benefits Reviews occur **post-implementation**, not just at project closure.
- ✅ The cycle is **iterative**: lessons from sustainment feed back into identification for the next project.
- ✅ Top three exam buzzwords: *Benefits Realization, Benefits Register, BMP, BRI (Benefit Realization Index), Sustainment*.
- ✅ Always end a Benefits-Management answer with a **decision statement** (Accept / Reject / Conditional Accept).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Project Planning # 📘 Module 2 — Project Planning ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Project Planning** is the disciplined, systematic process of defining, refining, and documenting the work to be performed by a software project. It is the foundational management activity in which the project manager establishes the *total scope of effort*, identifies the *controllable and uncontrollable risks*, and produces a coherent **Software Project Management Plan (SPMP)** that guides execution, monitoring, and control throughout the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). The KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT633) treats Project Planning as the central planning activity that bridges **Project Initiation** (Module 1) and **Project Execution & Closure** (Module 3). It is the bridge between *what* is to be built and *how, when, and by whom* it will be built. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The House Blueprint" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of Project Planning like building a house. > - Before laying a single brick, the architect drafts a **blueprint** (the WBS — Work Breakdown Structure). > - The contractor prepares a **time-schedule** saying "plastering starts on day 30" (the Project Schedule). > - The site engineer estimates **how many workers, how much cement, and what risks** (rain, delays) lie ahead (Resource & Risk Plans). > - The blueprint does **not** lay bricks — but **without it**, the house collapses or runs over budget. > > Similarly, the Software Project Management Plan (SPMP) does **not write a single line of code**, but **without it**, software projects routinely fail (Standish Group CHAOS Report consistently cites poor planning as the **#1 cause** of project failure). ### 1.3 Why Project Planning is Critical — KTU Board Perspective > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standish Group CHAOS Findings (industry benchmark often cited in KTU exams):** > - **31.1%** of software projects are cancelled before completion. > - **52.7%** cost **189% of their original estimates** on average. > - The single biggest remedy cited is **rigorous, up-front planning**. For KTU valuation, examiners expect students to demonstrate that planning directly addresses the **Iron Triangle** (also called the *Project Management Triangle*): | Constraint | What Planning Decides | |------------|----------------------| | **Scope** | What features will be delivered | | **Time** | When each feature will be delivered | | **Cost** | What manpower/money is needed | | **Quality** | What standards the product must meet | ### 1.4 Standard Metrics to Remember > [!TIP] > **Bold = frequently tested in KTU Part-A questions** > - **WBS (Work Breakdown Structure)** — Hierarchical decomposition of total work. > - **SPMP** — Software Project Management Plan (the formal output document). > - **COCOMO** — Constructive Cost Model (Boehm's estimation model). > - **PERT** — Program Evaluation Review Technique (3-time estimates, probabilistic). > - **CPM** — Critical Path Method (single-time, deterministic). > - **KLOC** — Thousand Lines of Code (size metric). > - **FP** — Function Points (size metric). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The 5-Step Project Planning Process (KTU High-Yield) According to **Roger S. Pressman** (industry-standard reference for KTU SE & PEITT633), project planning proceeds through these five disciplined steps: 1. **Step 1 — Scope Definition:** Establish the *boundaries* of the project. The project must be reviewed to ensure that it is well understood and that the boundaries are realistic. Deliverables, constraints, assumptions, and acceptance criteria are pinned down. 2. **Step 2 — Problem Decomposition:** Break the complex problem into smaller, manageable parts using the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**. 3. **Step 3 — Activity / Task Selection:** Identify the *activities* required to build each WBS component (also called *tasks* or *work packages*). 4. **Step 4 — Activity Network Construction:** Establish *precedence relationships* (which task must precede another). The output is the **Project Network Diagram** (activity-on-arrow AOA or activity-on-node AON). 5. **Step 5 — Schedule Development & Risk Refinement:** Estimate the time each task will take, compute the **critical path**, allocate resources, and refine risk plans. ### 2.2 Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) — The "Why" > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' behind WBS:** > A 10,000-hour project is psychologically unmanageable. A 100-hour work package is not. WBS exploits this cognitive limit — **the 8/80 Rule** states that no work package should be **less than 8 hours** or **more than 80 hours** of effort. The WBS organizes the project in a hierarchical, deliverable-oriented tree: ``` Project ├── Software Subsystem A │ ├── Module A1 │ │ ├── Design A1.1 │ │ ├── Code A1.2 │ │ └── Test A1.3 │ └── Module A2 └── Software Subsystem B ``` ### 2.3 Activity Networks — CPM vs PERT | Feature | CPM (Critical Path Method) | PERT (Program Evaluation Review Technique) | |---------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------| | **Time estimates** | Single deterministic estimate | **Three estimates** (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) | | **Origin** | DuPont (1957), construction | U.S. Navy (Polaris Missile, 1958) | | **Variance** | Not computed | Standard deviation & variance computed | | **Probabilistic?** | No | Yes — probability of meeting deadline is computable | | **Best for** | Well-understood repetitive tasks | R&D, novel software, uncertain environments | ### 2.4 PERT — The Weighted Mean Expected Time Formula > [!IMPORTANT] > **PERT Expected Time (TE) — Beta Distribution approximation:** > $$ T_E = \frac{T_O + 4T_M + T_P}{6} $$ > > - **$T_O$** = Optimistic estimate (best-case, no problems) > - **$T_M$** = Most Likely estimate (mode of the distribution) > - **$T_P$** = Pessimistic estimate (worst-case, everything goes wrong) ### 2.5 PERT Variance & Standard Deviation $$ \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{T_P - T_O}{6}\right)^2 $$ $$ \sigma = \frac{T_P - T_O}{6} $$ ### 2.6 CPM — Forward Pass & Backward Pass (Float/Slack) > [!NOTE] > **Definitions used in KTU valuation:** > - **ES (Early Start)** — Earliest time an activity can begin. > - **EF (Early Finish)** = ES + Duration > - **LF (Late Finish)** — Latest time an activity can finish **without delaying** the project. > - **LS (Late Start)** = LF − Duration > - **Total Float (Slack)** = LS − ES **OR** LF − EF > - **Critical Path** — The longest-duration path through the network. Activities on it have **Slack = 0**. ### 2.7 COCOMO (Basic Model — Boehm 1981) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Effort Equation (Basic COCOMO):** > $$ E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b \quad [\text{Person-Months}] $$ > $$ D = c \cdot E^d \quad [\text{Months}] $$ > $$ P = \frac{E}{D} \quad [\text{People}] $$ | Project Type | a | b | c | d | |--------------|---|---|---|---| | **Organic** (small, in-house) | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 | | **Semi-Detached** (medium) | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 | | **Embedded** (rigid constraints) | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 | ### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Save this table — it covers ~70% of numerical Part-B questions on Project Planning:** | # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | PERT Expected Time | $T_E = (T_O + 4T_M + T_P)/6$ | days/weeks | | 2 | PERT Variance | $\sigma^2 = [(T_P - T_O)/6]^2$ | days² | | 3 | PERT Std. Deviation | $\sigma = (T_P - T_O)/6$ | days | | 4 | Probability (Z-value) | $Z = (T_s - T_E)/\sigma$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Critical Path Duration | Sum of $T_E$ along longest path | days | | 6 | Total Float | $Slack = LS - ES$ | days | | 7 | COCOMO Effort | $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b$ | Person-Months | | 8 | COCOMO Duration | $D = c \cdot E^d$ | Months | | 9 | Productivity | $KLOC/E$ | KLOC/PM | | 10 | Effort by FP | $E = \frac{FP}{PROD}$ | Person-Months | ### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **IT Services (TCS, Infosys, Wipro):** WBS + Gantt charts are used in every fixed-price bid. - **Aerospace (ISRO, NASA):** PERT is mandatory for mission-critical software. - **Construction:** CPM is the legal standard in most public infrastructure tenders. - **Healthcare software:** Risk planning (FMEA-style) drives safety certifications. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — PERT Expected Time, Variance & Critical Path > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A software project has 5 activities A, B, C, D, E with the following data. Compute the expected duration, variance, and critical path. | Activity | Predecessor | $T_O$ | $T_M$ | $T_P$ | |----------|-------------|-------|-------|-------| | A | — | 2 | 4 | 6 | | B | — | 3 | 5 | 7 | | C | A | 1 | 2 | 3 | | D | A, B | 4 | 6 | 8 | | E | C, D | 2 | 3 | 4 | **Step 1 — Compute $T_E$ for every activity using $T_E = (T_O + 4T_M + T_P)/6$:** $$ T_E(A) = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days} $$ $$ T_E(B) = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days} $$ $$ T_E(C) = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{1 + 8 + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \text{ days} $$ $$ T_E(D) = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = \frac{4 + 24 + 8}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days} $$ $$ T_E(E) = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days} $$ **Step 2 — Identify all paths through the network (AOA form):** Paths available: **A → C → E** and **A → D → E** and **B → D → E**. - Path 1: A → C → E $= 4 + 2 + 3 = 9$ days - Path 2: A → D → E $= 4 + 6 + 3 = 13$ days - Path 3: B → D → E $= 5 + 6 + 3 = 14$ days **Step 3 — Identify the Critical Path (longest):** $$ \boxed{T_{project} = 14 \text{ days via Path B → D → E}} $$ **Step 4 — Variance of the critical path (sum of variances of activities on it):** $$ \sigma_A^2 = \left(\frac{6-2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{36} = 0.444 $$ $$ \sigma_B^2 = \left(\frac{7-3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444 $$ $$ \sigma_D^2 = \left(\frac{8-4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444 $$ $$ \sigma_E^2 = \left(\frac{4-2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{6}\right)^2 = 0.111 $$ $$ \sigma_{critical}^2 = \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_E^2 = 0.444 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.999 $$ $$ \sigma_{critical} = \sqrt{0.999} \approx 1.0 \text{ day} $$ **Step 5 — Probability of finishing in 16 days:** $$ Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma} = \frac{16 - 14}{1.0} = 2.0 $$ From the standard normal table: $P(Z \le 2.0) = 0.9772$, so **97.72% probability** of finishing in 16 days. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CPM Forward & Backward Pass > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Compute ES, EF, LS, LF, and Slack for the network from Example 1. **Step 1 — Forward Pass (compute ES and EF starting from Day 0):** $$ ES(A) = 0, \quad EF(A) = 0 + 4 = 4 $$ $$ ES(B) = 0, \quad EF(B) = 0 + 5 = 5 $$ $$ ES(C) = EF(A) = 4, \quad EF(C) = 4 + 2 = 6 $$ $$ ES(D) = \max(EF(A), EF(B)) = \max(4,5) = 5, \quad EF(D) = 5 + 6 = 11 $$ $$ ES(E) = \max(EF(C), EF(D)) = \max(6,11) = 11, \quad EF(E) = 11 + 3 = 14 $$ **Step 2 — Backward Pass (start from $LF(E) = 14$):** $$ LF(E) = 14, \quad LS(E) = 14 - 3 = 11 $$ $$ LF(D) = LS(E) = 11, \quad LS(D) = 11 - 6 = 5 $$ $$ LF(C) = LS(E) = 11, \quad LS(C) = 11 - 2 = 9 $$ $$ LF(B) = LS(D) = 5, \quad LS(B) = 5 - 5 = 0 $$ $$ LF(A) = \min(LS(C), LS(D)) = \min(9, 5) = 5, \quad LS(A) = 5 - 4 = 1 $$ **Step 3 — Slack Table:** | Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | Slack = LS − ES | Critical? | |----------|----------|----|----|----|----|-----------------|-----------| | A | 4 | 0 | 4 | 1 | 5 | **1** | No | | B | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | **0** | ✅ Yes | | C | 2 | 4 | 6 | 9 | 11 | **5** | No | | D | 6 | 5 | 11 | 5 | 11 | **0** | ✅ Yes | | E | 3 | 11 | 14 | 11 | 14 | **0** | ✅ Yes | **Step 4 — Critical Path confirmation:** $$ \boxed{\text{Critical Path: B → D → E, duration = 14 days}} $$ This matches our PERT computation — consistent! ✅ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — COCOMO Effort & Duration > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Estimate effort, duration, and team size for a **Semi-Detached** project of **8 KLOC**. **Step 1 — Look up constants for Semi-Detached:** From the formula sheet, $a = 3.0$, $b = 1.12$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.35$. **Step 2 — Compute Effort:** $$ E = 3.0 \times (8)^{1.12} $$ First, $(8)^{1.12} = e^{1.12 \cdot \ln 8} = e^{1.12 \cdot 2.0794} = e^{2.329} \approx 10.27$ $$ E \approx 3.0 \times 10.27 = 30.81 \text{ Person-Months} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Duration:** $$ D = 2.5 \times (30.81)^{0.35} $$ $(30.81)^{0.35} = e^{0.35 \cdot \ln 30.81} = e^{0.35 \cdot 3.428} = e^{1.200} \approx 3.32$ $$ D \approx 2.5 \times 3.32 = 8.30 \text{ Months} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Average Team Size:** $$ P = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{30.81}{8.30} \approx 3.71 \approx 4 \text{ people} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The 5-Step Project Planning Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Project Initiation from Module 1] A1[Step 1 Scope Definition] A2[Step 2 Problem Decomposition WBS] A3[Step 3 Activity Selection] A4[Step 4 Activity Network Construction] A5[Step 5 Schedule Development and Risk Refinement] A6[Software Project Management Plan SPMP] A7[Execution and Monitoring Module 3] A0 --> A1 A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 A5 --> A6 A6 --> A7 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide:** This is the linear top-down flow that Pressman prescribes. In practice, **iterations** (feedback loops) occur between every step. ### 4.2 Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD root[Software Project e.g. Online Banking System] n1[Subsystem A Authentication] n2[Subsystem B Transaction Engine] n3[Subsystem C Reporting] n11[WP1 Login Module Design] n12[WP2 Login Module Code] n13[WP3 Login Module Test] n21[WP4 Transaction Module Design] n22[WP5 Transaction Module Code] n23[WP6 Transaction Module Test] n31[WP7 Report Module Design] n32[WP8 Report Module Code] n33[WP9 Report Module Test] root --> n1 root --> n2 root --> n3 n1 --> n11 n1 --> n12 n1 --> n13 n2 --> n21 n2 --> n22 n2 --> n23 n3 --> n31 n3 --> n32 n3 --> n33 ``` ### 4.3 PERT/CPM Activity Network (AOA Style) — Critical Path B→D→E ```mermaid flowchart LR start([START]) endN([END 14 days]) A[A
TE=4 days
var=0.444] B[B
TE=5 days
var=0.444] C[C
TE=2 days
var=0.111] D[D
TE=6 days
var=0.444] E[E
TE=3 days
var=0.111] start --> A start --> B A --> C A --> D B --> D C --> E D --> E E --> endN classDef critical fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff; classDef noncritical fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px; class B,D,E critical; class A,C noncritical; ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual convention:** Critical-path activities are shaded **red** (zero slack). Non-critical activities are green. The longest weighted path through the network becomes the **project completion time**. ### 4.4 Gantt Chart Schematic — Sample Project ```mermaid gantt title Sample Software Project Schedule dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section Design Requirements Analysis :a1, 2025-01-01, 5d System Design :a2, after a1, 7d section Implementation Coding Module A :b1, after a2, 10d Coding Module B :b2, after a2, 12d section Testing Unit Testing :c1, after b1, 5d Integration Testing :c2, after c1, 7d UAT :c3, after c2, 4d ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the SPMP ```mermaid flowchart TD spmp[Software Project Management Plan SPMP] s1[1 Overview Section] s2[2 Project Organization] s3[3 Risk Management Plan] s4[4 Hardware and Software Resources] s5[5 Work Breakdown Structure WBS] s6[6 Project Schedule Gantt] s7[7 Monitoring and Reporting Mechanism] s8[8 Communication Plan] s9[9 Quality Assurance Plan] s10[10 Configuration Management Plan] s11[11 Cost Plan] spmp --> s1 spmp --> s2 spmp --> s3 spmp --> s4 spmp --> s5 spmp --> s6 spmp --> s7 spmp --> s8 spmp --> s9 spmp --> s10 spmp --> s11 ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask "List the components of an SPMP." Memorize the **11 sections above** — they appear in almost every End-Semester paper. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1.** *What is a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)? Mention the 8/80 rule.* **Model Answer:** A Work Breakdown Structure is a hierarchical, deliverable-oriented decomposition of the total work scope of a project into smaller, more manageable components called **work packages**. It transforms a complex project into a tree of clearly defined tasks. The **8/80 Rule** (heuristic for work-package sizing) states that no work package should be smaller than **8 hours** or larger than **80 hours** of effort. This ensures packages are granular enough to estimate accurately yet large enough to avoid micro-management overhead. > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q2.** *Differentiate between CPM and PERT.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | CPM | PERT | |--------|-----|------| | Time model | Deterministic (one estimate) | Probabilistic (three estimates) | | Activity duration variability | Assumed known & fixed | Allowed to vary | | Variance / Std. Dev. | Not computed | Computed using $\sigma^2 = [(T_P - T_O)/6]^2$ | | Best suited for | Construction, repetitive tasks | R&D, novel software | | Critical Path focus | Yes | Yes | | Allows probability of completion | No | Yes (via Z-value) | --- ### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(Module 2 — 14 Marks with internal choice)* --- #### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **five-step process of project planning** as proposed by Pressman, with a neat diagram. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A software project has the following activities. Compute the **expected time**, **variance**, **critical path**, and the **probability of completing within 22 days**. *(7 Marks)* | Activity | Predecessor | $T_O$ | $T_M$ | $T_P$ | |----------|-------------|-------|-------|-------| | A | — | 1 | 2 | 3 | | B | — | 2 | 3 | 10 | | C | A | 3 | 5 | 7 | | D | A | 1 | 4 | 7 | | E | B, C | 2 | 4 | 6 | | F | D | 3 | 4 | 11 | | G | E, F | 1 | 2 | 3 | --- #### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A **(a) [7 Marks]** *Five-Step Project Planning (Pressman):* 1. **Scope Definition:** Identify the boundaries, deliverables, constraints, assumptions, and acceptance criteria. Example: For an Online Banking System, scope includes login, fund transfer, statement generation — but not ATM hardware integration. 2. **Problem Decomposition (WBS):** Break total work into deliverable-oriented hierarchies using the **8/80 rule**. The WBS tree is built top-down. 3. **Activity Selection:** Identify the activities / tasks needed to build each WBS component. Activities are the *verbs* (design, code, test, integrate). 4. **Activity Network Construction:** Establish precedence relationships. Use either **Activity-on-Arrow (AOA)** or **Activity-on-Node (AON)** representation. Include **dummy activities** in AOA to maintain logical correctness. 5. **Schedule Development & Risk Refinement:** Estimate task durations, compute the critical path (CPM), refine with PERT three-time estimates if uncertainty exists, and finalize the **SPMP** document. > *(Draw the 5-step flowchart shown in Section 4.1 — 1 mark extra for diagram.)* **(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $T_E$ for all activities:** $$ T_E(A) = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 $$ $$ T_E(B) = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 $$ $$ T_E(C) = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 $$ $$ T_E(D) = \frac{1 + 4(4) + 7}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 $$ $$ T_E(E) = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 $$ $$ T_E(F) = \frac{3 + 4(4) + 11}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 $$ $$ T_E(G) = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 $$ **Step 2 — Compute variances:** $$ \sigma_A^2 = \left(\frac{3-1}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{36} = 0.111 $$ $$ \sigma_B^2 = \left(\frac{10-2}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{64}{36} = 1.778 $$ $$ \sigma_C^2 = \left(\frac{7-3}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{36} = 0.444 $$ $$ \sigma_D^2 = \left(\frac{7-1}{6}\right)^2 = 1.000 $$ $$ \sigma_E^2 = \left(\frac{6-2}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444 $$ $$ \sigma_F^2 = \left(\frac{11-3}{6}\right)^2 = 1.778 $$ $$ \sigma_G^2 = \left(\frac{3-1}{6}\right)^2 = 0.111 $$ **Step 3 — Identify all paths:** | Path | Activities | Sum of $T_E$ | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1 | A → C → E → G | $2 + 5 + 4 + 2 = 13$ | | 2 | A → D → F → G | $2 + 4 + 5 + 2 = 13$ | | 3 | B → E → G | $4 + 4 + 2 = 10$ | **Step 4 — Critical Path:** Multiple paths tie at 13 days. Choose the one with **higher variance** as the practical critical path (most risky). Path A → C → E → G has variance $0.111 + 0.444 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 1.110$. Path A → D → F → G has variance $0.111 + 1.000 + 1.778 + 0.111 = 3.000$. $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: A → D → F → G, expected duration = 13 days}}$$ $$ \sigma_{critical} = \sqrt{3.000} = 1.732 \text{ days} $$ **Step 5 — Probability of finishing in 22 days:** $$ Z = \frac{22 - 13}{1.732} = \frac{9}{1.732} = 5.196 $$ From standard normal tables, $P(Z \le 5.196) \approx 0.99999$ (≈ 100%). The project is **almost certain** to finish within 22 days. --- #### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **(a)** What is an **SPMP**? Describe **any six** of its major sections. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** For an **Organic-type** software project of **5 KLOC**, compute the effort, development time, and average team size using **Basic COCOMO**. *(7 Marks)* --- #### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B **(a) [7 Marks] — SPMP Definition & Components:** > **Software Project Management Plan (SPMP)** is the master planning document that captures the entire roadmap of a software project — scope, schedule, resources, risks, quality, and communication strategy. It serves as the single source of truth for all stakeholders. **Six Major Sections:** 1. **Overview / Introduction:** Project objectives, constraints, stakeholders, and success criteria. 2. **Project Organization:** Team structure, reporting hierarchy, roles & responsibilities (RACI chart). 3. **Risk Management Plan:** Identified risks, probability-impact matrix, mitigation strategies. 4. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS):** Hierarchical decomposition of project deliverables. 5. **Project Schedule:** Gantt chart, milestone dates, deliverables timeline. 6. **Monitoring & Control Mechanism:** How progress will be tracked (e.g., weekly status reports, earned-value analysis). 7. *(Bonus — Credit Section:)* Configuration Management Plan, Quality Assurance Plan, Communication Plan, Cost/Budget Plan. **(b) [7 Marks] — COCOMO Numerical Solution:** For an **Organic** project: $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$, with $KLOC = 5$. **Step 1 — Compute Effort:** $$ E = 2.4 \times (5)^{1.05} $$ $$ (5)^{1.05} = e^{1.05 \cdot \ln 5} = e^{1.05 \cdot 1.6094} = e^{1.690} \approx 5.42 $$ $$ E \approx 2.4 \times 5.42 = 13.01 \text{ Person-Months} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Duration:** $$ D = 2.5 \times (13.01)^{0.38} $$ $$ (13.01)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \cdot \ln 13.01} = e^{0.38 \cdot 2.566} = e^{0.975} \approx 2.65 $$ $$ D \approx 2.5 \times 2.65 = 6.63 \text{ Months} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Average Team Size:** $$ P = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{13.01}{6.63} \approx 1.96 \approx 2 \text{ people} $$ $$\boxed{E \approx 13 \text{ PM}, \quad D \approx 6.6 \text{ months}, \quad P \approx 2 \text{ people}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Wrong CPM Slack Formula:** Using *LF − EF* then writing it as *ES − LS* — they give the *same* number only by coincidence; you must justify with the formula *Slack = LS − ES*. > 2. **Missing the 1/6 weightage:** Students often compute PERT TE as the arithmetic mean $(T_O + T_M + T_P)/3$ instead of the weighted $(T_O + 4T_M + T_P)/6$ — **instant 2-mark loss**. > 3. **Forgetting Activity E's predecessors:** When A → C and A → D both merge before E, students often miss that E has *two* predecessors, leading to wrong ES(E). > 4. **COCOMO units confusion:** Effort is in *Person-Months*, not *Months*. Writing "13 Months" instead of "13 PM" loses 1 mark. > 5. **No "Critical Path" label on network diagram:** Always **redraw the critical path in bold/dashed lines** on the network — examiners award 2 marks for the visual identification. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Project Planning (Module 2)** - ⭐ **Project Planning is the BRIDGE** between initiation and execution — without it, projects fail (Standish CHAOS data). - ⭐ The **5 steps of Pressman** are: *Scope → WBS → Activities → Network → Schedule*. - ⭐ **WBS** is *deliverable-oriented*; **Activity list** is *verb-oriented*; they are NOT the same. - ⭐ The **8/80 rule** bounds work-package size at **8 to 80 hours**. - ⭐ **PERT formula:** $T_E = (T_O + 4T_M + T_P)/6$ — weightage 1:4:1 — NOT arithmetic mean! - ⭐ **PERT variance** uses only $T_O$ and $T_P$: $\sigma^2 = [(T_P - T_O)/6]^2$. - ⭐ **CPM** → single estimate, deterministic. **PERT** → three estimates, probabilistic. - ⭐ **Slack = LS − ES = LF − EF**. Critical-path activities have **Slack = 0**. - ⭐ **Forward pass** uses *max()* at merge nodes; **backward pass** uses *min()* at merge nodes. - ⭐ **COCOMO** Basic Model: $E = a \cdot KLOC^b$ and $D = c \cdot E^d$ — memorize the **3 project types & 6 constants**. - ⭐ **Productivity** = $KLOC / E$ (or $FP / PROD$). - ⭐ **SPMP has 11 standard sections** — know at least 6 by name for Part A. - ⭐ **Gantt chart** shows *time vs. activity*; **Network diagram** shows *logical dependencies*. - ⭐ Always **label the critical path visually** in red / bold on any network diagram. - ⭐ In Z-value computations, **$T_s > T_E$** gives positive Z (good — project ahead of risk).

topics.title: Stepwise Project Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stepwise Project Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepwise Project Planning (SPP)** is a hierarchical, top-down, multi-stage project planning methodology formalized by **Barry W. Boehm (1981)** in *Software Engineering Economics*. It is the systematic process of progressively refining a software project's goals, scope, functions, activities, resources, costs, and schedule — moving from broad strategic outlines down to granular, executable task definitions — before any actual coding or implementation begins.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEITT633 (Software Project Management)**, Stepwise Project Planning is treated as the **backbone planning framework** that bridges the gap between:
- **Strategic Feasibility Decisions** (Module 1 — feasibility analysis, cost-benefit, COCOMO estimates), and
- **Operational Project Execution** (Module 3+ — scheduling, risk, quality, people management).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Mapping:** Stepwise Project Planning occupies the central position in Module 2 because it is the *first deliverable-grade planning artifact* a software project manager produces after feasibility clearance.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine you are organizing a **destination wedding** in Kerala for 250 guests over 3 days.

You don't book the caterer, the orchestra, the decorator, and the priest on day one in isolation. Instead, you follow a **stepwise ladder**:

| Step | Real-World Action |
|------|-------------------|
| **Step 1** | Decide the *overall vision* — date, location, budget ceiling (Scope & Objectives) |
| **Step 2** | List *major functions* — catering, decoration, music, rituals, transport |
| **Step 3** | Break functions into *activities* — catering → menu selection, ingredient procurement, cooking, serving |
| **Step 4** | Estimate *size* — 250 guests × 4 meals = 1000 plates |
| **Step 5** | Estimate *resources* — 8 cooks, 4 servers, 2 drivers, 1 decorator team |
| **Step 6** | Estimate *cost* — ₹4.5 Lakhs catering + ₹2 L decoration = ₹6.5 L total |
| **Step 7** | Build *schedule* — Day 1: venue setup, Day 2: rituals, Day 3: reception |

**Stepwise Project Planning does exactly this** — but for software. Each step is a *refinement* of the previous one, adding **precision and detail**, ensuring that by Step 7 you have an executable, monitorable plan.

---

## 1.3 Why Stepwise Planning? (The Underlying Philosophy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** *"Planning is not a one-shot act; it is a progressive refinement process."*

A software project has the following inherent challenges:
- **Uncertainty** in requirements, technology, and team productivity.
- **Complexity** in decomposing a system into manageable units.
- **Interdependencies** between tasks, resources, and milestones.
- **Visibility** — management needs continuous updates on progress.

Stepwise Planning resolves these by:
1. **Reducing cognitive load** — managers handle one decision layer at a time.
2. **Enabling validation checkpoints** — each step produces a verifiable artifact.
3. **Allowing course correction** — wrong estimates at Step 4 can be revised before Step 7.
4. **Supporting communication** — different stakeholders consume different levels of detail.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome Mapping

| Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | How Stepwise Planning Addresses It |
|---------------------|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| **CO2** — Plan software projects using stepwise refinement | Apply / Analyze | Direct mapping — entire module is built on this |
| **CO3** — Estimate cost, effort, and schedule | Apply | Steps 4, 5, 6 produce these estimates |
| **CO4** — Manage scope, schedule, and resources | Analyze | Step 2 → 3 → 7 directly enables this |
| **CO5** — Apply risk and quality controls | Evaluate | Stepwise milestones act as risk gates |

---

## 1.5 The 7-Step Hierarchy at a Glance

```
STEP 7 ─► Project Plan (Integrated Document)
STEP 6 ─► Cost & Budget Estimate
STEP 5 ─► Resource Allocation
STEP 4 ─► Size & Effort Estimate
STEP 3 ─► Activity Decomposition (WBS)
STEP 2 ─► Function Identification
STEP 1 ─► Project Scope & Objectives
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Expectation:** Students MUST remember the **7-step order** and the **refinement direction (top-down)**. Examiners frequently test this in Part A (3-mark) questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### 🟦 STEP 1: Establish the Project Scope & Objectives

**Goal:** Define *what* is to be built and *why*.

**Inputs:**
- Feasibility Study Report (Module 1 output)
- System Requirements Specification (SRS)
- Stakeholder needs analysis

**Outputs:**
- **Project Scope Statement** — explicit description of deliverables, boundaries, exclusions.
- **SMART Objectives** — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.

**Why it matters:**
Without a bounded scope, every subsequent estimate is meaningless. Scope creep at Step 1 cascades into **20×–100× cost overruns** by Step 7.

**Key Activities:**
- Define deliverables (tangible + intangible).
- Identify constraints (budget, time, technology, regulatory).
- Define success criteria and acceptance gates.
- Get stakeholder sign-off → **Scope Baseline**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The scope baseline is the **reference point** against which all Change Requests (Module 3) are evaluated.

---

### 🟦 STEP 2: Identify the Project Functions (Function Decomposition)

**Goal:** Decompose the software system into its **major functional capabilities**.

**Inputs:** SRS, Use-Case Diagram, System Architecture sketch.

**Outputs:**
- **Function List / Function Hierarchy** — typically 10–50 top-level functions.

**Methods Used:**
- **Function Point Analysis (FPA)** external user functions (Inputs, Outputs, Inquiries, Files, Interfaces).
- **Use-Case Derivation** — each use-case → 1 function.
- **DFD Context Diagram** — bubbles become functions.

**Example (E-Commerce Portal):**
| # | Function | Source |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | User Registration | SRS §3.1 |
| 2 | Product Catalog Browsing | Use-Case UC-04 |
| 3 | Shopping Cart Management | Use-Case UC-07 |
| 4 | Payment Processing | SRS §3.5 |
| 5 | Order Tracking | Use-Case UC-12 |
| 6 | Admin Dashboard | SRS §3.8 |
| 7 | Email Notifications | SRS §3.10 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Functions" and not "Modules"?** Functions are *user-visible capabilities*; modules are *design-level artifacts*. Stepwise planning operates at the *capability* level to keep the plan technology-agnostic.

---

### 🟦 STEP 3: Determine the Activity Set (Activity Decomposition / WBS)

**Goal:** Break each function into **activities / tasks** that can be scheduled, estimated, and assigned.

**Inputs:** Function list from Step 2.

**Outputs:** **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** — a hierarchical decomposition of total work.

**Decomposition Rules:**
1. **100% Rule** — WBS must represent 100% of the work, not just project deliverables.
2. **8/80 Rule** — No work package < 8 hours or > 80 hours (Boehm's guideline).
3. **Mutually Exclusive** — no overlap between sibling tasks.
4. **Independently Assignable** — each leaf can be given to one person/team.

**Example WBS Snippet (Function: Payment Processing):**
```
3.0 Payment Processing
├── 3.1 Design Payment Gateway Interface
│   ├── 3.1.1 API contract design
│   ├── 3.1.2 Security protocol selection
│   └── 3.1.3 Failure-mode design
├── 3.2 Implement Card Validation
├── 3.3 Integrate with Bank API
├── 3.4 Implement Refund Workflow
├── 3.5 Unit Test Payment Module
└── 3.6 Integration Test with Cart & Order
```

**Why it matters:**
The WBS is the **single most important planning artifact**. Every estimate, schedule, and resource assignment in Steps 4–7 traces back to a WBS element.

---

### 🟦 STEP 4: Determine the Size & Effort Estimate

**Goal:** Quantify *how much* work each activity requires.

**Inputs:** WBS, historical productivity data, Function Points (Step 2 byproduct).

**Outputs:** Effort estimate (typically in **Person-Months**) for each WBS leaf.

**Estimation Techniques (used in combination):**

| Technique | Unit of Measure | When Used |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Lines of Code (LOC)** | KLOC | Algorithmic, large systems |
| **Function Points (FP)** | FP count | Business/ERP systems |
| **Use-Case Points (UCP)** | UCP | OO / UML projects |
| **COCOMO II** | Person-Months | Algorithmic + historical |
| **Expert Judgment / Delphi** | Person-Weeks | Early-stage, no metrics |
| **Analogous (Top-Down)** | Person-Months | When only similar past projects exist |

**Key Conversion Formulas (Critical for KTU):**

$$
1 \text{ Person-Month (PM)} = 152 \text{ Person-Hours}
$$
$$
1 \text{ Person-Year (PY)} = 12 \text{ PM} = 1{,}824 \text{ Person-Hours}
$$
$$
\text{Total Effort} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Effort}_i \text{ for WBS leaf } i)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 152 hours/month?** KTU follows Boehm's convention: 52 weeks/year ÷ 12 months × 5 days/week × 8 hours/day − holidays, vacation, training overhead ≈ **152 productive hours per person-month**.

---

### 🟦 STEP 5: Determine the Resource Estimates

**Goal:** Identify *who* and *what* is needed to execute each activity.

**Inputs:** WBS activity list, Effort estimates (Step 4), Resource pool inventory.

**Outputs:** **Resource Allocation Matrix (RAM)** — also called RACI chart.

**Resource Categories:**
1. **Human Resources** — developers, testers, designers, BA, PM.
2. **Hardware Resources** — servers, dev machines, mobile devices for testing.
3. **Software Resources** — IDEs, licenses (JetBrains, Oracle DB, AWS credits).
4. **Facility Resources** — office space, network bandwidth, VPN.
5. **Training Resources** — onboarding time, certification costs.

**Staffing Considerations (Boehm's 5 staffing patterns):**
| Pattern | Shape | Best For |
|---------|-------|----------|
| **Reducing** | High → Low | Maintenance projects |
| **Growing** | Low → High | Research / new-tech |
| **Stepped** | Low → High → Low | Phased delivery |
| **Slow-Ramp Buildup** | Very Low → High | New domain / apprenticeship |
| **Full-Time Buildup** | Steady High | Standard waterfall (most common) |

**Productivity Adjustment:**
$$
E_{\text{actual}} = E_{\text{nominal}} \times \prod_{j=1}^{m} P_j
$$

where $P_j$ are **Productivity Multipliers** (Boehm's COCOMO II factors):
- Personnel capability ($P_{cap}$) = **0.83 to 1.30**
- Tool/environment support ($P_{tool}$) = **0.85 to 1.20**
- Site/team cohesion ($P_{site}$) = **0.80 to 1.22**
- Schedule pressure ($P_{sched}$) = **1.00 to 1.43** (compression inflates effort)

---

### 🟦 STEP 6: Determine the Cost & Budget Estimate

**Goal:** Convert effort + resources into **monetary cost** and a **time-phased budget**.

**Inputs:** Effort (Step 4), Resources (Step 5), salary tables, infrastructure costs.

**Outputs:** **Cost Baseline**, **Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled (BCWS)**.

**Cost Components:**
$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{hardware}} + C_{\text{software}} + C_{\text{training}} + C_{\text{overhead}} + C_{\text{contingency}}
$$

**Labor Cost:**
$$
C_{\text{labor}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k \times R_k
$$

where:
- $E_k$ = effort of person category $k$ (in person-months)
- $R_k$ = loaded monthly salary of category $k$ (₹)
- $K$ = number of distinct personnel categories

**Loaded Salary Formula:**
$$
R_{\text{loaded}} = R_{\text{gross}} \times (1 + \text{benefits\%} + \text{overhead\%})
$$

**Typical KTU-Style Loaded Multiplier:** **1.4 to 2.0×** the gross salary (accounts for insurance, infrastructure, admin overhead).

**Contingency Reserve (Step 6 critical decision):**
$$
C_{\text{contingency}} = C_{\text{total}} \times \rho
$$

where $\rho$ = contingency percentage. Boehm recommends:
- $\rho = 10\%$ — high confidence, similar past project
- $\rho = 25\%$ — medium confidence
- $\rho = 50\%$ — low confidence, novel domain

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **management reserve** (separate, controlled by PMO) with **contingency reserve** (part of cost baseline, controlled by PM). Both are needed but serve different governance purposes.

---

### 🟦 STEP 7: Draw Up the Project Plan (Integration & Scheduling)

**Goal:** Assemble *all* previous outputs into a single, time-phased, executable **Project Plan** with a **critical path** and **milestone schedule**.

**Inputs:** WBS, Effort estimates, Resource assignments, Cost baseline.

**Outputs:**
- **Gantt Chart** (visual schedule)
- **Network Diagram** (PERT/CPM)
- **Milestone List**
- **Communication Plan**
- **Risk Register** (initial seed)
- **Project Plan Document (PPD)**

**Scheduling Equations (CPM — Critical Path Method):**

For each node $i$ in the activity-on-node network:

$$
\text{ES}_i = \max_{j \in \text{pred}(i)} (\text{EF}_j)
$$

$$
\text{EF}_i = \text{ES}_i + D_i
$$

$$
\text{LF}_i = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(i)} (\text{LS}_k)
$$

$$
\text{LS}_i = \text{LF}_i - D_i
$$

$$
\text{Slack}_i = \text{LS}_i - \text{ES}_i = \text{LF}_i - \text{EF}_i
$$

where:
- $D_i$ = duration of activity $i$
- $\text{ES}_i$ = Earliest Start, $\text{EF}_i$ = Earliest Finish
- $\text{LS}_i$ = Latest Start, $\text{LF}_i$ = Latest Finish
- **Critical Path** = set of activities where $\text{Slack}_i = 0$

**Project Duration:**
$$
T_{\text{project}} = \max_{i \in \text{end nodes}} (\text{EF}_i)
$$

**Earned Value Metrics (Step 7 monitoring baseline):**
$$
\text{PV (Planned Value)} = \text{BCWS} = \text{Budgeted cost for work scheduled to date}
$$

$$
\text{EV (Earned Value)} = \text{BCWP} = \text{Budgeted cost for work actually performed}
$$

$$
\text{AC (Actual Cost)} = \text{ACWP} = \text{Actual cost incurred}
$$

$$
\text{SV (Schedule Variance)} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}
$$

$$
\text{CV (Cost Variance)} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}
$$

$$
\text{SPI (Schedule Performance Index)} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}
$$

$$
\text{CPI (Cost Performance Index)} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear every semester in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $1 \text{ PM} = 152 \text{ hrs}$ | Boehm's person-month | hours |
| 2 | $C_{\text{labor}} = \sum E_k \times R_k$ | Total labor cost | ₹ / $ |
| 3 | $R_{\text{loaded}} = R_{\text{gross}} \times (1 + \text{ovh}\%)$ | Loaded salary | ₹/month |
| 4 | $C_{\text{contingency}} = C_{\text{total}} \times \rho$ | Risk reserve | ₹ |
| 5 | $E_{\text{actual}} = E_{\text{nom}} \times \prod P_j$ | Adjusted effort | PM |
| 6 | $\text{ES}_i = \max_{j \in \text{pred}(i)} \text{EF}_j$ | Earliest start | days |
| 7 | $\text{LS}_i = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(i)} \text{LF}_k - D_i$ | Latest start | days |
| 8 | $\text{Slack}_i = \text{LS}_i - \text{ES}_i$ | Float | days |
| 9 | $T_{\text{project}} = \max(\text{EF}_{\text{end}})$ | Total duration | days |
| 10 | $\text{CPI} = \text{EV} / \text{AC}$ | Cost efficiency | ratio |
| 11 | $\text{SPI} = \text{EV} / \text{PV}$ | Schedule efficiency | ratio |
| 12 | $\text{EAC} = \text{BAC} / \text{CPI}$ | Estimate at completion | ₹ |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- $\text{CPI} \geq 1.0$ → Under budget ✅
- $\text{CPI} < 1.0$ → Over budget ❌
- $\text{SPI} \geq 1.0$ → Ahead of schedule ✅
- $\text{SPI} < 1.0$ → Behind schedule ❌
- Productivity multipliers are always **multiplicative**, not additive.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 🌍

Stepwise Project Planning is used in:
- **Aerospace software** (Boeing, ISRO) — mission-critical, multi-decade projects.
- **Banking & Fintech** (Federal Bank, RBI systems) — regulatory + transaction-heavy.
- **Healthcare IT** (HIS, EHR systems) — patient-safety critical.
- **E-Governance projects** (Digital India, Kerala FIB) — public-funded, audit-heavy.
- **Telecom** (5G rollout software) — large-scale, geographically distributed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Trend (2024+):** Modern Agile/Scrum projects still use a *compressed version* of stepwise planning — Release Planning (analogous to Step 7), Iteration Planning (analogous to Step 3–4). Boehm's framework remains the **conceptual ancestor** of all modern planning methods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU Style Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** This is a typical KTU Part B 14-mark question (sub-part style). Walk through it as you would write in your exam.

### 🟨 Problem Statement

**SmartLib — A Library Management System for KTU**
A university wants to develop *SmartLib*, a library automation system. The feasibility study estimates the project at **6 calendar months** with a team of **4 developers**. Historical data shows the team's nominal productivity is **0.6 PM per FP** for design+code+test phases combined.

The function point analysis yields the following counts (unadjusted):

| Function Type | Count | Weight |
|---------------|-------|--------|
| External Inputs (EI) | 12 | 4 |
| External Outputs (EO) | 8 | 5 |
| External Inquiries (EQ) | 5 | 4 |
| Internal Logical Files (ILF) | 4 | 10 |
| External Interface Files (EIF) | 3 | 7 |

**General System Characteristics (GSC) total = 38** (out of 14 standard factors, each rated 0–5).

**Other data:**
- Average fully loaded monthly salary per developer = **₹80,000**
- 1 PM = 152 person-hours
- Required contingency reserve = **20%**
- Boehm's productivity multipliers for this project: $P_{cap}=1.10$, $P_{tool}=0.90$, $P_{site}=1.05$, $P_{sched}=1.00$

**Tasks:**
(a) Compute the **adjusted Function Point count** and **total effort estimate** (with productivity multipliers). (7 marks)
(b) Compute the **total project cost** including contingency, and determine the **per-function cost** assuming all 32 functions are equally weighted. (7 marks)

---

### 🟨 PART (a) Solution — Function Points & Effort

#### 🟢 Step 1: Compute Unadjusted Function Points (UFP)

Using the standard FPA weight table:

$$
\text{UFP} = \sum_{i} (\text{Count}_i \times \text{Weight}_i)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UFP} &= (\text{EI} \times 4) + (\text{EO} \times 5) + (\text{EQ} \times 4) \\
&\quad + (\text{ILF} \times 10) + (\text{EIF} \times 7)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UFP} &= (12 \times 4) + (8 \times 5) + (5 \times 4) + (4 \times 10) + (3 \times 7) \\
&= 48 + 40 + 20 + 40 + 21 \\
&= 169 \text{ FP (unadjusted)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each external input contributes 4 FPs (simple), each output 5, etc. Summing across all function types yields total unadjusted complexity.

---

#### 🟢 Step 2: Compute Value Adjustment Factor (VAF)

Boehm's VAF formula:

$$
\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{j=1}^{14} F_j
$$

Given total GSC $\sum F_j = 38$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAF} &= 0.65 + 0.01 \times 38 \\
&= 0.65 + 0.38 \\
&= 1.03
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A GSC of 38 indicates the system is *slightly more complex than average*. VAF ranges from **0.65 (extremely simple)** to **1.35 (extremely complex)**. Our 1.03 is near baseline.

---

#### 🟢 Step 3: Compute Adjusted Function Points (AFP / DFP)

$$
\text{FP}_{\text{adjusted}} = \text{UFP} \times \text{VAF}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FP}_{\text{adjusted}} &= 169 \times 1.03 \\
&= 174.07 \text{ FP} \\
&\approx 174 \text{ FP}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Adjustment accounts for application complexity, performance, reusability, etc. Our system is moderately complex → small 3% inflation.

---

#### 🟢 Step 4: Compute Nominal Effort

Using the given productivity rate of **0.6 PM per FP**:

$$
E_{\text{nominal}} = \text{FP}_{\text{adjusted}} \times \text{Productivity}_{\text{rate}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{nominal}} &= 174 \times 0.6 \\
&= 104.4 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** At 0.6 PM per FP, ~104 person-months are needed. This is the *baseline* before any team adjustment.

---

#### 🟢 Step 5: Apply Productivity Multipliers

$$
E_{\text{actual}} = E_{\text{nominal}} \times P_{cap} \times P_{tool} \times P_{site} \times P_{sched}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{actual}} &= 104.4 \times 1.10 \times 0.90 \times 1.05 \times 1.00 \\
&= 104.4 \times (1.10 \times 0.90 \times 1.05) \\
&= 104.4 \times 1.0395 \\
&\approx 108.52 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The team is *capable* (+10%), but *tools are slightly inadequate* (-10%), and *site coordination costs* add +5%. Net: **+3.95%** effort.

---

#### 🟢 Step 6: Validate Against Schedule

Planned effort based on team size and duration:

$$
E_{\text{planned}} = 4 \text{ devs} \times 6 \text{ months} = 24 \text{ PM}
$$

Compare:

$$
\frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{planned}}} = \frac{108.52}{24} \approx 4.52
$$

> **Logic & Examiner Note:** $E_{\text{actual}} \gg E_{\text{planned}}$ → the plan is **drastically under-resourced**. Either: increase team to 18 devs (impractical), extend duration to 27 months, or reduce scope. This is a classic KTU exam "discuss" follow-up.

---

### 🟨 PART (b) Solution — Cost Computation

#### 🟢 Step 1: Labor Cost (using adjusted effort)

$$
C_{\text{labor}} = E_{\text{actual}} \times R_{\text{monthly}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{labor}} &= 108.52 \times 80{,}000 \\
&= ₹87{,}81{,}600 \\
&\approx ₹87.82 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each person-month costs ₹80,000 (already loaded). Multiply by total person-months → total labor cost.

---

#### 🟢 Step 2: Contingency Reserve

$$
C_{\text{contingency}} = 0.20 \times C_{\text{labor}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{contingency}} &= 0.20 \times 87{,}81{,}600 \\
&= ₹17{,}56{,}320 \\
&\approx ₹17.56 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### 🟢 Step 3: Total Project Cost

Assuming only labor + contingency (no separate hardware/software costs given):

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{contingency}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= 87{,}81{,}600 + 17{,}56{,}320 \\
&= ₹1{,}05{,}37{,}920 \\
&\approx ₹1.05 \text{ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### 🟢 Step 4: Per-Function Cost

Total identified functions = **32** (assumed from the function list of the problem).

$$
C_{\text{per function}} = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{functions}}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{per function}} &= \frac{1{,}05{,}37{,}920}{32} \\
&= ₹3{,}29{,}310 \\
&\approx ₹3.29 \text{ Lakhs / function}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 🟨 Examiner's Valuation Checklist (Model Answer Blueprint)

| Checkpoint | Marks Allocated |
|------------|-----------------|
| Correct UFP calculation with all 5 components | 2 |
| Correct VAF formula and value | 1 |
| AFP = UFP × VAF | 1 |
| Nominal effort with units | 1 |
| Productivity multiplier application | 1 |
| Correct labor cost | 1 |
| Contingency reserve calculation | 1 |
| Total cost | 1 |
| Per-function cost | 1 |
| Schedule feasibility comment | 2 |
| **Total** | **11 + 3** |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Planning Context | Best Stepwise Variant | Rationale |
|------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| **New domain, vague requirements** | Heavily front-loaded Step 1–3 | Risk reduction |
| **Maintenance project** | Lighter Step 2, heavy Step 4–7 historical data | Use past productivity |
| **Offshore outsourced project** | Heavy Step 5 (site multiplier dominant) | Coordination cost |
| **Agile environment** | Compress all 7 steps into Release Planning | Iterative refinement |
| **Compliance/regulated (FDA, RBI)** | Heavy Step 1 + Step 6 (audit trail) | Documentation critical |

---

## 3.3 Common Numerical Traps in KTU Exams

> [!WARNING]
> **Trap 1:** Confusing **UFP** and **AFP** — KTU examiners frequently give partial credit only for both shown.
>
> **Trap 2:** Forgetting to convert productivity rate units. If given as "0.6 PM per FP" but effort needed in hours → multiply by 152.
>
> **Trap 3:** Adding productivity multipliers instead of multiplying.
>
> **Trap 4:** Computing contingency on labor only, vs on total cost including hardware. Read the question wording carefully.

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define Stepwise Project Planning. List the seven steps in correct order.**

**Model Answer:**

Stepwise Project Planning is a hierarchical, top-down project planning methodology (formalized by **Barry Boehm, 1981**) that progressively refines a software project's scope, functions, activities, resources, costs, and schedule across **seven sequential stages**. Each step produces a verifiable artifact that feeds the next, reducing estimation uncertainty.

The **seven steps in order** are:

1. **Establish the Project Scope and Objectives** — define what is to be built.
2. **Identify the Project Functions** — capability-level decomposition.
3. **Determine the Activity Set / WBS** — break functions into tasks.
4. **Determine the Size and Effort Estimate** — quantify work (FP, LOC, COCOMO).
5. **Determine the Resource Estimates** — staff, hardware, software.
6. **Determine the Cost and Budget Estimate** — convert effort to ₹/$ + reserve.
7. **Draw up the Project Plan** — integrate schedule, budget, WBS, risk register.

> **Remember:** Direction is **top-down (coarse → fine)**.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between UFP (Unadjusted Function Points) and AFP (Adjusted Function Points). Why is the Value Adjustment Factor (VAF) needed?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | UFP | AFP |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Definition** | Raw count of functions × weights | UFP adjusted for system complexity |
| **Formula** | $\sum (\text{Count} \times \text{Weight})$ | $\text{UFP} \times \text{VAF}$ |
| **Complexity** | Ignores it | Reflects 14 GSC factors |
| **Range** | 0 to unbounded | Same magnitude, adjusted |
| **Use** | Initial sizing | Effort estimation, productivity calc |

**Need for VAF:**
UFP treats all functions as equally complex, which is unrealistic. VAF incorporates **14 General System Characteristics** (data communications, performance, reusability, etc.) using the formula:

$$
\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{j=1}^{14} F_j
$$

VAF ranges from **0.65 (very simple)** to **1.35 (very complex)**, with 1.0 being average. Without VAF, effort estimates would be inaccurate by ±30–40%.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]`

**Case Study: MediCare+ Hospital Management System**

A multi-specialty hospital wants to develop **MediCare+**, a web-based HMS. The Function Point Analysis yielded the following unadjusted counts:

| Function Type | Count | Weight |
|---------------|-------|--------|
| EI | 15 | 4 |
| EO | 10 | 5 |
| EQ | 7 | 4 |
| ILF | 6 | 10 |
| EIF | 4 | 7 |

The GSC total = **42**. Historical productivity = **0.55 PM/FP**. Loaded monthly salary = **₹90,000 per developer**. Contingency = **18%**. Multipliers: $P_{cap} = 1.15$, $P_{tool} = 0.85$, $P_{site} = 1.08$, $P_{sched} = 1.00$.

**(a)** Apply the seven steps of Stepwise Project Planning to derive the **adjusted Function Points** and the **final effort estimate** in person-months. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **total labor cost**, the **contingency reserve**, and the **grand total project cost**. Also find the cost per Function Point. (7 marks)

---

#### 📝 MODEL SOLUTION

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks Detailed Solution

**Step 1: Compute UFP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UFP} &= (15 \times 4) + (10 \times 5) + (7 \times 4) + (6 \times 10) + (4 \times 7) \\
&= 60 + 50 + 28 + 60 + 28 \\
&= 226 \text{ FP}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Compute VAF**

$$
\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 42 = 0.65 + 0.42 = 1.07
$$

> *(1 mark)*

**Step 3: Compute AFP**

$$
\text{AFP} = 226 \times 1.07 = 241.82 \approx 242 \text{ FP}
$$

> *(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Nominal Effort**

$$
E_{\text{nom}} = 242 \times 0.55 = 133.1 \text{ PM}
$$

> *(1 mark)*

**Step 5: Productivity Adjustment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{actual}} &= 133.1 \times 1.15 \times 0.85 \times 1.08 \times 1.00 \\
&= 133.1 \times 1.0563 \\
&\approx 140.59 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(2 marks — 1 for setup, 1 for final value with units)*

**Step 6: Summary of Steps 1–5 (from Stepwise Planning perspective)**

- **Step 1 Scope:** HMS covering OPD, IPD, Pharmacy, Lab, Billing.
- **Step 2 Functions:** Patient Registration, OPD Management, IPD Admission, Lab Order, Pharmacy Dispense, Billing, Reporting, etc.
- **Step 3 WBS:** Each function broken into 6–10 activities (Design, Develop, Test, Deploy).
- **Step 4 Effort:** 140.59 PM (just computed).
- **Step 5 Resources:** Team composition (BA, Devs, QA, DBAs, UI/UX).

> *(1 mark)*

---

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks Detailed Solution

**Step 1: Labor Cost**

$$
C_{\text{labor}} = 140.59 \times 90{,}000 = ₹1{,}26{,}53{,}100 \approx ₹1.265 \text{ Crore}
$$

> *(2 marks)*

**Step 2: Contingency Reserve**

$$
C_{\text{cont}} = 0.18 \times 1{,}26{,}53{,}100 = ₹22{,}77{,}558 \approx ₹22.78 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

> *(2 marks)*

**Step 3: Grand Total Cost**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 1{,}26{,}53{,}100 + 22{,}77{,}558 = ₹1{,}49{,}30{,}658 \approx ₹1.493 \text{ Crore}
$$

> *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 4: Cost per Function Point**

$$
\text{Cost/FP} = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{\text{AFP}} = \frac{1{,}49{,}30{,}658}{242} \approx ₹61{,}697 \text{ per FP}
$$

> *(1.5 marks)*

**Sanity Check:** Industry typical cost per FP in India ranges from ₹40,000–80,000 → our ₹61,697 is **within normal range** ✅.

---

### ✏️ **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B]`

**Conceptual + Numerical: Stepwise Planning Methodology**

**(a)** Explain the **seven steps of Stepwise Project Planning** in detail with the inputs and outputs of each step. Discuss why the process is **iterative and not strictly linear** despite being sequential. (7 marks)

**(b)** Given the following network diagram for a software module (activities A through G), draw the **forward and backward pass**, identify the **critical path**, and compute the **project duration**.

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| A | — | 4 |
| B | — | 6 |
| C | A | 5 |
| D | A | 7 |
| E | B | 4 |
| F | C, D | 6 |
| G | E, F | 5 |

(7 marks)

---

#### 📝 MODEL SOLUTION

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks Conceptual Answer

The seven steps with inputs/outputs are:

| Step | Input | Output |
|------|-------|--------|
| 1. Scope & Objectives | Feasibility Report, SRS | Scope Baseline, SMART objectives |
| 2. Identify Functions | SRS, Use Cases | Function List (10–50 functions) |
| 3. Activity Decomposition (WBS) | Function List | WBS with work packages |
| 4. Size & Effort Estimate | WBS, historical data | Person-Month estimates |
| 5. Resource Estimates | WBS, effort, HR pool | Resource Allocation Matrix (RAM) |
| 6. Cost & Budget Estimate | Effort, salary tables | Cost Baseline, BCWS |
| 7. Project Plan | All prior outputs | Gantt Chart, Network Diagram, PPD |

**Why Iterative Despite Being Sequential?**

1. **Discovery at later steps** — Step 3 may uncover hidden sub-activities that change Step 2's function list (e.g., "Payment" splits into "Card", "UPI", "Wallet").
2. **Estimation revisions** — Step 4 reveals effort > Step 6 budget → revisit Step 1 scope.
3. **Resource conflicts** — Step 5 may find a skill gap → trigger Step 6 retraining cost.
4. **Stakeholder feedback** — Sign-off at Step 1 may change after seeing Step 7 schedule.
5. **Risk realization** — A risk identified in Step 7 monitoring may force Step 1 re-baselining.
6. **Boehm's "Spiral" influence** — Each step allows a refinement loop back, making it **spiral planning**, not waterfall.

> *(Distribute 7 marks as: 4 for table content, 3 for "iterative" justification with 3+ valid points.)*

---

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks CPM Numerical Solution

**Forward Pass** (compute ES, EF):

$$
\text{ES}_A = 0, \quad \text{EF}_A = 0 + 4 = 4
$$
$$
\text{ES}_B = 0, \quad \text{EF}_B = 0 + 6 = 6
$$
$$
\text{ES}_C = \text{EF}_A = 4, \quad \text{EF}_C = 4 + 5 = 9
$$
$$
\text{ES}_D = \text{EF}_A = 4, \quad \text{EF}_D = 4 + 7 = 11
$$
$$
\text{ES}_E = \text{EF}_B = 6, \quad \text{EF}_E = 6 + 4 = 10
$$
$$
\text{ES}_F = \max(\text{EF}_C, \text{EF}_D) = \max(9, 11) = 11, \quad \text{EF}_F = 11 + 6 = 17
$$
$$
\text{ES}_G = \max(\text{EF}_E, \text{EF}_F) = \max(10, 17) = 17, \quad \text{EF}_G = 17 + 5 = 22
$$

**Project Duration: $T_{\text{project}} = \text{EF}_G = 22$ days**

**Backward Pass** (compute LF, LS from end node G):

$$
\text{LF}_G = 22, \quad \text{LS}_G = 22 - 5 = 17
$$
$$
\text{LF}_F = \text{LS}_G = 17, \quad \text{LS}_F = 17 - 6 = 11
$$
$$
\text{LF}_E = \text{LS}_G = 17, \quad \text{LS}_E = 17 - 4 = 13
$$
$$
\text{LF}_D = \text{LS}_F = 11, \quad \text{LS}_D = 11 - 7 = 4
$$
$$
\text{LF}_C = \text{LS}_F = 11, \quad \text{LS}_C = 11 - 5 = 6
$$
$$
\text{LF}_A = \min(\text{LS}_C, \text{LS}_D) = \min(6, 4) = 4, \quad \text{LS}_A = 4 - 4 = 0
$$
$$
\text{LF}_B = \text{LS}_E = 13, \quad \text{LS}_B = 13 - 6 = 7
$$

**Slack Calculation:**

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | Slack | Critical? |
|----------|----------|----|----|----|----|-------|-----------|
| A | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | ✅ Yes |
| B | 6 | 0 | 6 | 7 | 13 | 7 | No |
| C | 5 | 4 | 9 | 6 | 11 | 2 | No |
| D | 7 | 4 | 11 | 4 | 11 | **0** | ✅ Yes |
| E | 4 | 6 | 10 | 13 | 17 | 7 | No |
| F | 6 | 11 | 17 | 11 | 17 | **0** | ✅ Yes |
| G | 5 | 17 | 22 | 17 | 22 | **0** | ✅ Yes |

**Critical Path: A → D → F → G**

**Project Duration = 22 days**

> *(2 marks network + 3 marks forward + 1.5 marks backward + 0.5 marks critical path)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Stepwise Planning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting VAF** — Many students compute UFP and stop. **−2 to −3 marks** penalty.
> 2. **Adding multipliers instead of multiplying** — $E \times (1.10 + 0.90 + ...)$ instead of $E \times 1.10 \times 0.90 \times ...$ → wrong answer.
> 3. **Wrong VAF range assumption** — Some students assume VAF ranges 0–1 instead of **0.65–1.35**.
> 4. **Not stating units** — "143.5" without "PM" or "days" loses marks in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Misidentifying critical path** — Only one activity having slack = 0 ≠ critical path. **ALL activities on the path** must have zero slack.
> 6. **Confusing "functions" with "modules"** — Module = design decision; Function = user-visible capability.
> 7. **Skipping Step 1 in numericals** — KTU may deduct 1 mark if scope statement is missing before estimation begins.

---

## 🔖 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Stepwise Project Planning**

- ✅ **Seven Steps** (in order): Scope → Functions → WBS → Effort → Resources → Cost → Plan.
- ✅ **Boehm (1981)** is the originator — write his name if asked for credit.
- ✅ **Direction:** Top-down, coarse-to-fine.
- ✅ **UFP formula:** Sum of (Count × Weight) for EI, EO, EQ, ILF, EIF.
- ✅ **VAF formula:** $0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_j$ (range 0.65–1.35).
- ✅ **AFP** = UFP × VAF.
- ✅ **1 Person-Month** = 152 person-hours.
- ✅ **Productivity multipliers are multiplicative**, not additive.
- ✅ **WBS 100% Rule** — captures all work, not only deliverables.
- ✅ **WBS 8/80 Rule** — no work package < 8 hrs or > 80 hrs.
- ✅ **CPM Critical Path** = activities where Slack = 0.
- ✅ **Forward pass:** ES = max(EF of predecessors), EF = ES + D.
- ✅ **Backward pass:** LF = min(LS of successors), LS = LF − D.
- ✅ **CPI = EV / AC**; **SPI = EV / PV**; both ≥ 1.0 = healthy.
- ✅ **Contingency reserve** = % of cost baseline, controlled by PM.
- ✅ **Management reserve** = separate, controlled by PMO/sponsor.
- ✅ **Stepwise is iterative** despite being sequential — Boehm's spiral influence.
- ✅ **In Agile**, the 7 steps compress into Release + Iteration Planning.
- ✅ **Industry use:** Aerospace, Banking, Healthcare, E-Gov, Telecom.
- ✅ **KTU examiner loves:** "Explain the seven steps with inputs/outputs" — prepare this answer verbatim.

---

> **END OF NOTES — STEPWISE PROJECT PLANNING | PEITT633 Module 2 | KTU 2024 SCHEME**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Estimation** is the disciplined, analytical process of forecasting the essential attributes of a software project — namely **size**, **effort**, **duration (schedule)**, and **cost** — using historical data, mathematical models, expert judgment, and decomposition techniques, conducted *before* significant resources are committed to the project.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Software Estimation is treated as a **continuous, iterative activity** (not a one-time event) that feeds directly into the **Project Management Plan**, the **Risk Register**, and the **Earned Value Management** baseline. It is one of the most critical knowledge areas in the **PMBOK / IEEE / KTU-aligned** project management body of knowledge.

The four primary estimation parameters in software engineering are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit of Measure |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Size** | $S$ | KLOC (Kilo Lines of Code) or Function Points (FP) |
| **Effort** | $E$ | Person-Months (PM) |
| **Duration** | $D$ | Months |
| **Cost** | $C$ | Currency units (₹, $, etc.) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "House-Building" Analogy** — Imagine you want to build a house in Kerala. Before laying the first brick, you need to estimate: How many bricks? How many workers? How many days? How much money? You wouldn't quote a price without measuring the plot, checking the soil, and listing the rooms. **Software Estimation is exactly this — but for an "invisible" product made of code instead of bricks.** The catch? Software is *logical*, not physical, so the "measurements" must be taken from requirements documents, use cases, and past similar projects.

Another way to think about it:

- **"Estimate"** ≠ **"Exact value"**. An estimate is a *probabilistic range* with an associated confidence level. KTU examiners reward students who acknowledge that estimation is inherently **uncertain** and use techniques to **reduce** that uncertainty.
- Good estimation is the difference between a project that finishes on time and one that overruns by 200%.

## 1.3 Why Software Estimation is Hard (KTU Highlight)

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Invisible Product" Problem** — Unlike a bridge or a building, software has *no physical mass* until it is built. You cannot weigh it, measure it with a tape, or photograph it. Therefore, every estimation technique must **proxy** the real product using surrogate measures like LOC, FP, or Use-Case Points.

The four fundamental reasons estimation is challenging:

1. **Problem Understanding Gap** — Requirements are often ambiguous, incomplete, or change during estimation.
2. **Access to Historical Data** — Accurate estimation requires comparable past projects, which may not exist.
3. **Human Bias** — Optimism bias, anchoring, and political pressure distort estimates.
4. **Technology Change Rate** — New frameworks, languages, and tools make historical data less applicable.

## 1.4 The Estimation Process (High-Level Flow)

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames estimation as a four-stage iterative loop:

1. **Estimate the Size** of the software (LOC or FP).
2. **Estimate the Effort** using a model (COCOMO, COCOMO II) or expert technique.
3. **Estimate the Duration** (schedule) using staffing profiles and calendars.
4. **Estimate the Cost** by applying labor rates to effort.

Each stage produces numbers that **feed forward** into the next, and results are **refined** as the project progresses through its phases (Concept → Feasibility → Detailed Design → Construction).

---
